title: Poem 10
text: As is well documented, Emily Dickinson's poems were edited in these early editions by her friends, better to fit the conventions of the times. In particular, her dashes, often small enough to appear as dots, became commas and semi–colons.

title: Poem 11
text: In the second series of poems published, a facsimile of her handwritten poem which her editors titled "Renunciation" is given, and comparing this to the printed version gives a flavor of the changes made in these early editions.

title: Poem 20
text: MABEL LOOMIS TODD and T.W. HIGGINSON

title: Poem 21
text: PREFACE.

title: Poem 22
text: The verses of Emily Dickinson belong emphatically to what Emerson long since called "the Poetry of the Portfolio,"–something produced absolutely without the thought of publication, and solely by way of expression of the writer's own mind. Such verse must inevitably forfeit whatever advantage lies in the discipline of public criticism and the enforced conformity to accepted ways. On the other hand, it may often gain something through the habit of freedom and the unconventional utterance of daring thoughts. In the case of the present author, there was absolutely no choice in the matter; she must write thus, or not at all. A recluse by temperament and habit, literally spending years without setting her foot beyond the doorstep, and many more years during which her walks were strictly limited to her father's grounds, she habitually concealed her mind, like her person, from all but a very few friends; and it was with great difficulty that she was persuaded to print, during her lifetime, three or four poems. Yet she wrote verses in great abundance; and though brought curiously indifferent to all conventional rules, had yet a rigorous literary standard of her own, and often altered a word many times to suit an ear which had its own tenacious fastidiousness.

title: Poem 23
text: Miss Dickinson was born in Amherst, Mass., Dec. 10, 1830, and died there May 15, 1886. Her father, Hon. Edward Dickinson, was the leading lawyer of Amherst, and was treasurer of the well–known college there situated. It was his custom once a year to hold a large reception at his house, attended by all the families connected with the institution and by the leading people of the town. On these occasions his daughter Emily emerged from her wonted retirement and did her part as gracious hostess; nor would any one have known from her manner, I have been told, that this was not a daily occurrence. The annual occasion once past, she withdrew again into her seclusion, and except for a very few friends was as invisible to the world as if she had dwelt in a nunnery. For myself, although I had corresponded with her for many years, I saw her but twice face to face, and brought away the impression of something as unique and remote as Undine or Mignon or Thekla.

title: Poem 24
text: This selection from her poems is published to meet the desire of her personal friends, and especially of her surviving sister. It is believed that the thoughtful reader will find in these pages a quality more suggestive of the poetry of William Blake than of anything to be elsewhere found,–flashes of wholly original and profound insight into nature and life; words and phrases exhibiting an extraordinary vividness of descriptive and imaginative power, yet often set in a seemingly whimsical or even rugged frame. They are here published as they were written, with very few and superficial changes; although it is fair to say that the titles have been assigned, almost invariably, by the editors. In many cases these verses will seem to the reader like poetry torn up by the roots, with rain and dew and earth still clinging to them, giving a freshness and a fragrance not otherwise to be conveyed. In other cases, as in the few poems of shipwreck or of mental conflict, we can only wonder at the gift of vivid imagination by which this recluse woman can delineate, by a few touches, the very crises of physical or mental struggle. And sometimes again we catch glimpses of a lyric strain, sustained perhaps but for a line or two at a time, and making the reader regret its sudden cessation. But the main quality of these poems is that of extraordinary grasp and insight, uttered with an uneven vigor sometimes exasperating, seemingly wayward, but really unsought and inevitable. After all, when a thought takes one's breath away, a lesson on grammar seems an impertinence. As Ruskin wrote in his earlier and better days, "No weight nor mass nor beauty of execution can outweigh one grain or fragment of thought."

title: Poem 25
text: ––Thomas Wentworth Higginson

title: This is my letter to the world,
text:
That never wrote to me, –
The simple news that Nature told,
With tender majesty.

title: Her message is committed
Poem 28
text: I. LIFE.

title: Poem 29
text: I.

title: Poem 30
text: SUCCESS.

title: [Published in "A Masque of Poets"
text:
at the request of "H.H.," the author's
fellow–townswoman and friend.]

title: Success is counted sweetest
text:
By those who ne'er succeed.
To comprehend a nectar
Requires sorest need.

title: Not one of all the purple host
text:
Who took the flag to–day
Can tell the definition,
So clear, of victory,

title: As he, defeated, dying,
text:
On whose forbidden ear
The distant strains of triumph
Break, agonized and clear!

title: Poem 35
text: II.

title: Our share of night to bear,
text:
Our share of morning,
Our blank in bliss to fill,
Our blank in scorning.

title: Here a star, and there a star,
text:
Some lose their way.
Here a mist, and there a mist,
Afterwards – day!

title: Poem 38
text: III.

title: Poem 39
text: ROUGE ET NOIR.

title: Soul, wilt thou toss again?
text:
By just such a hazard
Hundreds have lost, indeed,
But tens have won an all.

title: Angels' breathless ballot
Poem 42
text: IV.

title: Poem 43
text: ROUGE GAGNE.

title: 'T is so much joy! 'T is so much joy!
text:
If I should fail, what poverty!
And yet, as poor as I
Have ventured all upon a throw;
Have gained! Yes! Hesitated so
This side the victory!

title: Life is but life, and death but death!
text:
Bliss is but bliss, and breath but breath!
And if, indeed, I fail,
At least to know the worst is sweet.
Defeat means nothing but defeat,
No drearier can prevail!

title: And if I gain, – oh, gun at sea,
text:
Oh, bells that in the steeples be,
At first repeat it slow!
For heaven is a different thing
Conjectured, and waked sudden in,
And might o'erwhelm me so!

title: Poem 47
text: V.

title: Glee! The great storm is over!
Ring, for the scant salvation!
text:
Toll, for the bonnie souls, –
Neighbor and friend and bridegroom,
Spinning upon the shoals!

title: How they will tell the shipwreck
text:
When winter shakes the door,
Till the children ask, "But the forty?
Did they come back no more?"

title: Then a silence suffuses the story,
Poem 52
text: VI.

title: If I can stop one heart from breaking,
text:
I shall not live in vain;
If I can ease one life the aching,
Or cool one pain,
Or help one fainting robin
Unto his nest again,
I shall not live in vain.

title: Poem 54
text: VII.

title: Poem 55
text: ALMOST!

title: Within my reach!
text:
I could have touched!
I might have chanced that way!
Soft sauntered through the village,
Sauntered as soft away!
So unsuspected violets
Within the fields lie low,
Too late for striving fingers
That passed, an hour ago.

title: Poem 57
text: VIII.

title: A wounded deer leaps highest,
The smitten rock that gushes,
Mirth is the mail of anguish,
text:
In which it cautions arm,
Lest anybody spy the blood
And "You're hurt" exclaim!

title: Poem 61
text: IX.

title: The heart asks pleasure first,
And then, to go to sleep;
text:
And then, if it should be
The will of its Inquisitor,
The liberty to die.

title: Poem 64
text: X.

title: Poem 65
text: IN A LIBRARY.

title: A precious, mouldering pleasure 't is
His venerable hand to take,
text:
And warming in our own,
A passage back, or two, to make
To times when he was young.

title: His quaint opinions to inspect,
What interested scholars most,
When Sappho was a living girl,
text:
And Beatrice wore
The gown that Dante deified.
Facts, centuries before,

title: He traverses familiar,
His presence is enchantment,
Poem 73
text: XI.

title: Much madness is divinest sense
text:
To a discerning eye;
Much sense the starkest madness.
'T is the majority
In this, as all, prevails.
Assent, and you are sane;
Demur, – you're straightway dangerous,
And handled with a chain.

title: Poem 75
text: XII.

title: I asked no other thing,
Brazil? He twirled a button,
text:
Without a glance my way:
"But, madam, is there nothing else
That we can show to–day?"

title: Poem 78
text: XIII.

title: Poem 79
text: EXCLUSION.

title: The soul selects her own society,
Unmoved, she notes the chariot's pausing
I've known her from an ample nation
Poem 83
text: XIV.

title: Poem 84
text: THE SECRET.

title: Some things that fly there be, –
text:
Birds, hours, the bumble–bee:
Of these no elegy.

title: Some things that stay there be, –
text:
Grief, hills, eternity:
Nor this behooveth me.

title: There are, that resting, rise.
text:
Can I expound the skies?
How still the riddle lies!

title: Poem 88
text: XV.

title: Poem 89
text: THE LONELY HOUSE.

title: I know some lonely houses off the road
text:
A robber 'd like the look of, –
Wooden barred,
And windows hanging low,
Inviting to
A portico,
Where two could creep:
One hand the tools,
The other peep
To make sure all's asleep.
Old–fashioned eyes,
Not easy to surprise!

title: How orderly the kitchen 'd look by night,
text:
With just a clock, –
But they could gag the tick,
And mice won't bark;
And so the walls don't tell,
None will.

title: A pair of spectacles ajar just stir –
text:
An almanac's aware.
Was it the mat winked,
Or a nervous star?
The moon slides down the stair
To see who's there.

title: There's plunder, – where?
text:
Tankard, or spoon,
Earring, or stone,
A watch, some ancient brooch
To match the grandmamma,
Staid sleeping there.

title: Day rattles, too,
text:
Stealth's slow;
The sun has got as far
As the third sycamore.
Screams chanticleer,
"Who's there?"
And echoes, trains away,
Sneer – "Where?"
While the old couple, just astir,
Fancy the sunrise left the door ajar!

title: Poem 95
text: XVI.

title: To fight aloud is very brave,
text:
But gallanter, I know,
Who charge within the bosom,
The cavalry of woe.

title: Who win, and nations do not see,
text:
Who fall, and none observe,
Whose dying eyes no country
Regards with patriot love.

title: We trust, in plumed procession,
text:
For such the angels go,
Rank after rank, with even feet
And uniforms of snow.

title: Poem 99
text: XVII.

title: Poem 100
text: DAWN.

title: When night is almost done,
text:
And sunrise grows so near
That we can touch the spaces,
It 's time to smooth the hair

title: And get the dimples ready,
text:
And wonder we could care
For that old faded midnight
That frightened but an hour.

title: Poem 103
text: XVIII.

title: Poem 104
text: THE BOOK OF MARTYRS.

title: Read, sweet, how others strove,
text:
Till we are stouter;
What they renounced,
Till we are less afraid;
How many times they bore
The faithful witness,
Till we are helped,
As if a kingdom cared!

title: Read then of faith
text:
That shone above the fagot;
Clear strains of hymn
The river could not drown;
Brave names of men
And celestial women,
Passed out of record
Into renown!

title: Poem 107
text: XIX.

title: Poem 108
text: THE MYSTERY OF PAIN.

title: Pain has an element of blank;
text:
It cannot recollect
When it began, or if there were
A day when it was not.

title: It has no future but itself,
text:
Its infinite realms contain
Its past, enlightened to perceive
New periods of pain.

title: Poem 111
text: XX.

title: I taste a liquor never brewed,
Inebriate of air am I,
text:
And debauchee of dew,
Reeling, through endless summer days,
From inns of molten blue.

title: When landlords turn the drunken bee
text:
Out of the foxglove's door,
When butterflies renounce their drams,
I shall but drink the more!

title: Till seraphs swing their snowy hats,
text:
And saints to windows run,
To see the little tippler
Leaning against the sun!

title: Poem 116
text: XXI.

title: Poem 117
text: A BOOK.

title: He ate and drank the precious words,
text:
His spirit grew robust;
He knew no more that he was poor,
Nor that his frame was dust.
He danced along the dingy days,
And this bequest of wings
Was but a book. What liberty
A loosened spirit brings!

title: Poem 119
text: XXII.

title: I had no time to hate, because
text:
The grave would hinder me,
And life was not so ample I
Could finish enmity.

title: Nor had I time to love; but since
text:
Some industry must be,
The little toil of love, I thought,
Was large enough for me.

title: Poem 122
text: XXIII.

title: Poem 123
text: UNRETURNING.

title: 'T was such a little, little boat
text:
That toddled down the bay!
'T was such a gallant, gallant sea
That beckoned it away!

title: 'T was such a greedy, greedy wave
Poem 126
text: XXIV.

title: Whether my bark went down at sea,
By what mystic mooring
text:
She is held to–day, –
This is the errand of the eye
Out upon the bay.

title: Poem 129
text: XXV.

title: Belshazzar had a letter, –
text:
He never had but one;
Belshazzar's correspondent
Concluded and begun
In that immortal copy
The conscience of us all
Can read without its glasses
On revelation's wall.

title: Poem 131
text: XXVI.

title: The brain within its groove
text:
Runs evenly and true;
But let a splinter swerve,
'T were easier for you
To put the water back
When floods have slit the hills,
And scooped a turnpike for themselves,
And blotted out the mills!

title: Poem 133
text: II. LOVE.

title: Poem 134
text: I.

title: Poem 135
text: MINE.

title: Mine by the right of the white election!
text:
Mine by the royal seal!
Mine by the sign in the scarlet prison
Bars cannot conceal!

title: Mine, here in vision and in veto!
text:
Mine, by the grave's repeal
Titled, confirmed, – delirious charter!
Mine, while the ages steal!

title: Poem 138
text: II.

title: Poem 139
text: BEQUEST.

title: You left me, sweet, two legacies, –
You left me boundaries of pain
text:
Capacious as the sea,
Between eternity and time,
Your consciousness and me.

title: Poem 142
text: III.

title: Alter? When the hills do.
text:
Falter? When the sun
Question if his glory
Be the perfect one.

title: Surfeit? When the daffodil
text:
Doth of the dew:
Even as herself, O friend!
I will of you!

title: Poem 145
text: IV.

title: Poem 146
text: SUSPENSE.

title: Elysium is as far as to
text:
The very nearest room,
If in that room a friend await
Felicity or doom.

title: What fortitude the soul contains,
text:
That it can so endure
The accent of a coming foot,
The opening of a door!

title: Poem 149
text: V.

title: Poem 150
text: SURRENDER.

title: Doubt me, my dim companion!
text:
Why, God would be content
With but a fraction of the love
Poured thee without a stint.
The whole of me, forever,
What more the woman can, –
Say quick, that I may dower thee
With last delight I own!

title: It cannot be my spirit,
text:
For that was thine before;
I ceded all of dust I knew, –
What opulence the more
Had I, a humble maiden,
Whose farthest of degree
Was that she might,
Some distant heaven,
Dwell timidly with thee!

title: Poem 153
text: VI.

title: If you were coming in the fall,
text:
I'd brush the summer by
With half a smile and half a spurn,
As housewives do a fly.

title: If I could see you in a year,
text:
I'd wind the months in balls,
And put them each in separate drawers,
Until their time befalls.

title: If only centuries delayed,
text:
I'd count them on my hand,
Subtracting till my fingers dropped
Into Van Diemen's land.

title: If certain, when this life was out,
text:
That yours and mine should be,
I'd toss it yonder like a rind,
And taste eternity.

title: But now, all ignorant of the length
text:
Of time's uncertain wing,
It goads me, like the goblin bee,
That will not state its sting.

title: Poem 159
text: VII.

title: Poem 160
text: WITH A FLOWER.

title: I hide myself within my flower,
text:
That wearing on your breast,
You, unsuspecting, wear me too –
And angels know the rest.

title: I hide myself within my flower,
text:
That, fading from your vase,
You, unsuspecting, feel for me
Almost a loneliness.

title: Poem 163
text: VIII.

title: Poem 164
text: PROOF.

title: That I did always love,
text:
I bring thee proof:
That till I loved
I did not love enough.

title: That I shall love alway,
text:
I offer thee
That love is life,
And life hath immortality.

title: This, dost thou doubt, sweet?
text:
Then have I
Nothing to show
But Calvary.

title: Poem 168
text: IX.

title: Have you got a brook in your little heart,
text:
Where bashful flowers blow,
And blushing birds go down to drink,
And shadows tremble so?

title: And nobody knows, so still it flows,
Then look out for the little brook in March,
text:
When the rivers overflow,
And the snows come hurrying from the hills,
And the bridges often go.

title: And later, in August it may be,
text:
When the meadows parching lie,
Beware, lest this little brook of life
Some burning noon go dry!

title: Poem 173
text: X.

title: Poem 174
text: TRANSPLANTED.

title: As if some little Arctic flower,
text:
Upon the polar hem,
Went wandering down the latitudes,
Until it puzzled came
To continents of summer,
To firmaments of sun,
To strange, bright crowds of flowers,
And birds of foreign tongue!
I say, as if this little flower
To Eden wandered in –
What then? Why, nothing, only,
Your inference therefrom!

title: Poem 176
text: XI.

title: Poem 177
text: THE OUTLET.

title: My river runs to thee:
text: Blue sea, wilt welcome me?

title: My river waits reply.
text: Oh sea, look graciously!

title: I'll fetch thee brooks
text: From spotted nooks, –

title: Say, sea,
text: Take me!

title: Poem 182
text: XII.

title: Poem 183
text: IN VAIN.

title: I cannot live with you,
text:
It would be life,
And life is over there
Behind the shelf

title: The sexton keeps the key to,
text:
Putting up
Our life, his porcelain,
Like a cup

title: Discarded of the housewife,
I could not die with you,
text:
For one must wait
To shut the other's gaze down, –
You could not.

title: And I, could I stand by
text:
And see you freeze,
Without my right of frost,
Death's privilege?

title: Nor could I rise with you,
text:
Because your face
Would put out Jesus',
That new grace

title: Glow plain and foreign
text:
On my homesick eye,
Except that you, than he
Shone closer by.

title: They'd judge us – how?
Because you saturated sight,
text:
And I had no more eyes
For sordid excellence
As Paradise.

title: And were you lost, I would be,
text:
Though my name
Rang loudest
On the heavenly fame.

title: And were you saved,
text:
And I condemned to be
Where you were not,
That self were hell to me.

title: So we must keep apart,
text:
You there, I here,
With just the door ajar
That oceans are,
And prayer,
And that pale sustenance,
Despair!

title: Poem 196
text: XIII.

title: Poem 197
text: RENUNCIATION.

title: Poem 198
text: First page of Renunciation Second page of Renunciation Third page of Renunciation Fourth page of Renunciation

title: There came a day at summer's full
The sun, as common, went abroad,
text:
The flowers, accustomed, blew,
As if no soul the solstice passed
That maketh all things new.

title: The time was scarce profaned by speech;
text:
The symbol of a word
Was needless, as at sacrament
The wardrobe of our Lord.

title: Each was to each the sealed church,
text:
Permitted to commune this time,
Lest we too awkward show
At supper of the Lamb.

title: The hours slid fast, as hours will,
And so, when all the time had failed,
text:
Without external sound,
Each bound the other's crucifix,
We gave no other bond.

title: Sufficient troth that we shall rise –
text:
Deposed, at length, the grave –
To that new marriage, justified
Through Calvaries of Love!

title: Poem 206
text: XIV.

title: Poem 207
text: LOVE'S BAPTISM.

title: I'm ceded, I've stopped being theirs;
text:
The name they dropped upon my face
With water, in the country church,
Is finished using now,
And they can put it with my dolls,
My childhood, and the string of spools
I've finished threading too.

title: Baptized before without the choice,
text:
But this time consciously, of grace
Unto supremest name,
Called to my full, the crescent dropped,
Existence's whole arc filled up
With one small diadem.

title: My second rank, too small the first,
text:
Crowned, crowing on my father's breast,
A half unconscious queen;
But this time, adequate, erect,
With will to choose or to reject.
And I choose – just a throne.

title: Poem 211
text: XV.

title: Poem 212
text: RESURRECTION.

title: 'T was a long parting, but the time
These fleshless lovers met,
text:
A heaven in a gaze,
A heaven of heavens, the privilege
Of one another's eyes.

title: No lifetime set on them,
text:
Apparelled as the new
Unborn, except they had beheld,
Born everlasting now.

title: Was bridal e'er like this?
text:
A paradise, the host,
And cherubim and seraphim
The most familiar guest.

title: Poem 217
text: XVI.

title: Poem 218
text: APOCALYPSE.

title: I'm wife; I've finished that,
How odd the girl's life looks
text:
Behind this soft eclipse!
I think that earth seems so
To those in heaven now.

title: This being comfort, then
Poem 222
text: XVII.

title: Poem 223
text: THE WIFE.

title: She rose to his requirement, dropped
text:
The playthings of her life
To take the honorable work
Of woman and of wife.

title: If aught she missed in her new day
text:
Of amplitude, or awe,
Or first prospective, or the gold
In using wore away,

title: It lay unmentioned, as the sea
text:
Develops pearl and weed,
But only to himself is known
The fathoms they abide.

title: Poem 227
text: XVIII.

title: Poem 228
text: APOTHEOSIS.

title: Come slowly, Eden!
text:
Lips unused to thee,
Bashful, sip thy jasmines,
As the fainting bee,

title: Reaching late his flower,
text:
Round her chamber hums,
Counts his nectars – enters,
And is lost in balms!

title: Poem 231
text: III. NATURE.

title: Poem 232
text: I.

title: New feet within my garden go,
New children play upon the green,
Poem 235
text: II.

title: Poem 236
text: MAY–FLOWER.

title: Pink, small, and punctual,
text:
Aromatic, low,
Covert in April,
Candid in May,

title: Dear to the moss,
text:
Known by the knoll,
Next to the robin
In every human soul.

title: Bold little beauty,
text:
Bedecked with thee,
Nature forswears
Antiquity.

title: Poem 240
text: III.

title: Poem 241
text: WHY?

title: The murmur of a bee
text:
A witchcraft yieldeth me.
If any ask me why,
'T were easier to die
Than tell.

title: The red upon the hill
text:
Taketh away my will;
If anybody sneer,
Take care, for God is here,
That's all.

title: The breaking of the day
text:
Addeth to my degree;
If any ask me how,
Artist, who drew me so,
Must tell!

title: Poem 245
text: IV.

title: Perhaps you'd like to buy a flower?
text:
But I could never sell.
If you would like to borrow
Until the daffodil

title: Unties her yellow bonnet
text:
Beneath the village door,
Until the bees, from clover rows
Their hock and sherry draw,

title: Why, I will lend until just then,
text: But not an hour more!

title: Poem 249
text: V.

title: The pedigree of honey
Poem 251
text: VI.

title: Poem 252
text: A SERVICE OF SONG.

title: Some keep the Sabbath going to church;
text:
I keep it staying at home,
With a bobolink for a chorister,
And an orchard for a dome.

title: Some keep the Sabbath in surplice;
text:
I just wear my wings,
And instead of tolling the bell for church,
Our little sexton sings.

title: God preaches, – a noted clergyman, –
Poem 256
text: VII.

title: The bee is not afraid of me,
The brooks laugh louder when I come,
text:
The breezes madder play.
Wherefore, mine eyes, thy silver mists?
Wherefore, O summer's day?

title: Poem 259
text: VIII.

title: Poem 260
text: SUMMER'S ARMIES.

title: Some rainbow coming from the fair!
text:
Some vision of the world Cashmere
I confidently see!
Or else a peacock's purple train,
Feather by feather, on the plain
Fritters itself away!

title: The dreamy butterflies bestir,
text:
Lethargic pools resume the whir
Of last year's sundered tune.
From some old fortress on the sun
Baronial bees march, one by one,
In murmuring platoon!

title: The robins stand as thick to–day
text:
As flakes of snow stood yesterday,
On fence and roof and twig.
The orchis binds her feather on
For her old lover, Don the Sun,
Revisiting the bog!

title: Without commander, countless, still,
text:
The regiment of wood and hill
In bright detachment stand.
Behold! Whose multitudes are these?
The children of whose turbaned seas,
Or what Circassian land?

title: Poem 265
text: IX.

title: Poem 266
text: THE GRASS.

title: The grass so little has to do, –
text:
A sphere of simple green,
With only butterflies to brood,
And bees to entertain,

title: And stir all day to pretty tunes
And thread the dews all night, like pearls,
text:
And make itself so fine, –
A duchess were too common
For such a noticing.

title: And even when it dies, to pass
text:
In odors so divine,
As lowly spices gone to sleep,
Or amulets of pine.

title: And then to dwell in sovereign barns,
text:
And dream the days away, –
The grass so little has to do,
I wish I were the hay!

title: Poem 272
text: X.

title: A little road not made of man,
text:
Enabled of the eye,
Accessible to thill of bee,
Or cart of butterfly.

title: If town it have, beyond itself,
Poem 275
text: XI.

title: Poem 276
text: SUMMER SHOWER.

title: A drop fell on the apple tree,
A few went out to help the brook,
text:
That went to help the sea.
Myself conjectured, Were they pearls,
What necklaces could be!

title: The dust replaced in hoisted roads,
The breezes brought dejected lutes,
Poem 281
text: XII.

title: Poem 282
text: PSALM OF THE DAY.

title: A something in a summer's day,
text:
As slow her flambeaux burn away,
Which solemnizes me.

title: A something in a summer's noon, –
text:
An azure depth, a wordless tune,
Transcending ecstasy.

title: And still within a summer's night
text:
A something so transporting bright,
I clap my hands to see;

title: Then veil my too inspecting face,
text:
Lest such a subtle, shimmering grace
Flutter too far for me.

title: The wizard–fingers never rest,
text:
The purple brook within the breast
Still chafes its narrow bed;

title: Still rears the East her amber flag,
text:
Guides still the sun along the crag
His caravan of red,

title: Like flowers that heard the tale of dews,
text:
But never deemed the dripping prize
Awaited their low brows;

title: Or bees, that thought the summer's name
text:
Some rumor of delirium
No summer could for them;

title: Or Arctic creature, dimly stirred
text:
By tropic hint, – some travelled bird
Imported to the wood;

title: Or wind's bright signal to the ear,
text:
Making that homely and severe,
Contented, known, before

title: The heaven unexpected came,
text:
To lives that thought their worshipping
A too presumptuous psalm.

title: Poem 294
text: XIII.

title: Poem 295
text: THE SEA OF SUNSET.

title: This is the land the sunset washes,
Night after night her purple traffic
Poem 298
text: XIV.

title: Poem 299
text: PURPLE CLOVER.

title: There is a flower that bees prefer,
And whatsoever insect pass,
text:
A honey bears away
Proportioned to his several dearth
And her capacity.

title: Her face is rounder than the moon,
text:
And ruddier than the gown
Of orchis in the pasture,
Or rhododendron worn.

title: She doth not wait for June;
text:
Before the world is green
Her sturdy little countenance
Against the wind is seen,

title: Contending with the grass,
text:
Near kinsman to herself,
For privilege of sod and sun,
Sweet litigants for life.

title: And when the hills are full,
text:
And newer fashions blow,
Doth not retract a single spice
For pang of jealousy.

title: Her public is the noon,
text:
Her providence the sun,
Her progress by the bee proclaimed
In sovereign, swerveless tune.

title: The bravest of the host,
text:
Surrendering the last,
Nor even of defeat aware
When cancelled by the frost.

title: Poem 308
text: XV.

title: Poem 309
text: THE BEE.

title: Like trains of cars on tracks of plush
text:
I hear the level bee:
A jar across the flowers goes,
Their velvet masonry

title: Withstands until the sweet assault
text:
Their chivalry consumes,
While he, victorious, tilts away
To vanquish other blooms.

title: His feet are shod with gauze,
His labor is a chant,
Poem 314
text: XVI.

title: Presentiment is that long shadow on the lawn
Poem 316
text: XVII.

title: As children bid the guest good–night,
text:
And then reluctant turn,
My flowers raise their pretty lips,
Then put their nightgowns on.

title: As children caper when they wake,
text:
Merry that it is morn,
My flowers from a hundred cribs
Will peep, and prance again.

title: Poem 319
text: XVIII.

title: Angels in the early morning
text:
May be seen the dews among,
Stooping, plucking, smiling, flying:
Do the buds to them belong?

title: Angels when the sun is hottest
Poem 322
text: XIX.

title: So bashful when I spied her,
So breathless till I passed her,
text:
So helpless when I turned
And bore her, struggling, blushing,
Her simple haunts beyond!

title: For whom I robbed the dingle,
text:
For whom betrayed the dell,
Many will doubtless ask me,
But I shall never tell!

title: Poem 326
text: XX.

title: Poem 327
text: TWO WORLDS.

title: It makes no difference abroad,
text:
The seasons fit the same,
The mornings blossom into noons,
And split their pods of flame.

title: Wild–flowers kindle in the woods,
Auto–da–fe and judgment
Poem 331
text: XXI.

title: Poem 332
text: THE MOUNTAIN.

title: The mountain sat upon the plain
text:
In his eternal chair,
His observation omnifold,
His inquest everywhere.

title: The seasons prayed around his knees,
text:
Like children round a sire:
Grandfather of the days is he,
Of dawn the ancestor.

title: Poem 335
text: XXII.

title: Poem 336
text: A DAY.

title: I'll tell you how the sun rose, –
text:
A ribbon at a time.
The steeples swam in amethyst,
The news like squirrels ran.

title: The hills untied their bonnets,
text:
The bobolinks begun.
Then I said softly to myself,
"That must have been the sun!"

title: But how he set, I know not.
text:
There seemed a purple stile
Which little yellow boys and girls
Were climbing all the while

title: Till when they reached the other side,
text:
A dominie in gray
Put gently up the evening bars,
And led the flock away.

title: Poem 341
text: XXIII.

title: The butterfly's assumption–gown,
text:
In chrysoprase apartments hung,
This afternoon put on.

title: How condescending to descend,
text:
And be of buttercups the friend
In a New England town!

title: Poem 344
text: XXIV.

title: Poem 345
text: THE WIND.

title: Of all the sounds despatched abroad,
text:
There's not a charge to me
Like that old measure in the boughs,
That phraseless melody

title: The wind does, working like a hand
text:
Whose fingers brush the sky,
Then quiver down, with tufts of tune
Permitted gods and me.

title: When winds go round and round in bands,
text:
And thrum upon the door,
And birds take places overhead,
To bear them orchestra,

title: I crave him grace, of summer boughs,
text:
If such an outcast be,
He never heard that fleshless chant
Rise solemn in the tree,

title: As if some caravan of sound
text:
On deserts, in the sky,
Had broken rank,
Then knit, and passed
In seamless company.

title: Poem 351
text: XXV.

title: Poem 352
text: DEATH AND LIFE.

title: Apparently with no surprise
text:
To any happy flower,
The frost beheads it at its play
In accidental power.
The blond assassin passes on,
The sun proceeds unmoved
To measure off another day
For an approving God.

title: Poem 354
text: XXVI.

title: 'T was later when the summer went
text:
Than when the cricket came,
And yet we knew that gentle clock
Meant nought but going home.

title: 'T was sooner when the cricket went
text:
Than when the winter came,
Yet that pathetic pendulum
Keeps esoteric time.

title: Poem 357
text: XXVII.

title: Poem 358
text: INDIAN SUMMER.

title: These are the days when birds come back,
text:
A very few, a bird or two,
To take a backward look.

title: These are the days when skies put on
text:
The old, old sophistries of June, –
A blue and gold mistake.

title: Oh, fraud that cannot cheat the bee,
text:
Almost thy plausibility
Induces my belief,

title: Till ranks of seeds their witness bear,
text:
And softly through the altered air
Hurries a timid leaf!

title: Oh, sacrament of summer days,
text:
Oh, last communion in the haze,
Permit a child to join,

title: Thy sacred emblems to partake,
text:
Thy consecrated bread to break,
Taste thine immortal wine!

title: Poem 365
text: XXVIII.

title: Poem 366
text: AUTUMN.

title: The morns are meeker than they were,
The maple wears a gayer scarf,
text:
The field a scarlet gown.
Lest I should be old–fashioned,
I'll put a trinket on.

title: Poem 369
text: XXIX.

title: Poem 370
text: BECLOUDED.

title: The sky is low, the clouds are mean,
text:
A travelling flake of snow
Across a barn or through a rut
Debates if it will go.

title: A narrow wind complains all day
Poem 373
text: XXX.

title: Poem 374
text: THE HEMLOCK.

title: I think the hemlock likes to stand
That men must slake in wilderness,
text:
Or in the desert cloy, –
An instinct for the hoar, the bald,
Lapland's necessity.

title: The hemlock's nature thrives on cold;
text:
The gnash of northern winds
Is sweetest nutriment to him,
His best Norwegian wines.

title: To satin races he is nought;
text:
But children on the Don
Beneath his tabernacles play,
And Dnieper wrestlers run.

title: Poem 379
text: XXXI.

title: There's a certain slant of light,
text:
On winter afternoons,
That oppresses, like the weight
Of cathedral tunes.

title: Heavenly hurt it gives us;
text:
We can find no scar,
But internal difference
Where the meanings are.

title: None may teach it anything,
text:
'T is the seal, despair, –
An imperial affliction
Sent us of the air.

title: When it comes, the landscape listens,
Poem 384
text: IV. TIME AND ETERNITY.

title: Poem 385
text: I.

title: One dignity delays for all,
text:
One mitred afternoon.
None can avoid this purple,
None evade this crown.

title: Coach it insures, and footmen,
What dignified attendants,
text:
What service when we pause!
How loyally at parting
Their hundred hats they raise!

title: How pomp surpassing ermine,
text:
When simple you and I
Present our meek escutcheon,
And claim the rank to die!

title: Poem 390
text: II.

title: Poem 391
text: TOO LATE.

title: Delayed till she had ceased to know,
text:
Delayed till in its vest of snow
Her loving bosom lay.
An hour behind the fleeting breath,
Later by just an hour than death, –
Oh, lagging yesterday!

title: Could she have guessed that it would be;
text:
Could but a crier of the glee
Have climbed the distant hill;
Had not the bliss so slow a pace, –
Who knows but this surrendered face
Were undefeated still?

title: Oh, if there may departing be
text:
Any forgot by victory
In her imperial round,
Show them this meek apparelled thing,
That could not stop to be a king,
Doubtful if it be crowned!

title: Poem 395
text: III.

title: Poem 396
text: ASTRA CASTRA.

title: Departed to the judgment,
The flesh surrendered, cancelled,
Poem 399
text: IV.

title: Safe in their alabaster chambers,
text:
Untouched by morning and untouched by noon,
Sleep the meek members of the resurrection,
Rafter of satin, and roof of stone.

title: Light laughs the breeze in her castle of sunshine;
Grand go the years in the crescent above them;
text:
Worlds scoop their arcs, and firmaments row,
Diadems drop and Doges surrender,
Soundless as dots on a disk of snow.

title: Poem 403
text: V.

title: On this long storm the rainbow rose,
The birds rose smiling in their nests,
The quiet nonchalance of death
Poem 407
text: VI.

title: Poem 408
text: FROM THE CHRYSALIS.

title: My cocoon tightens, colors tease,
A power of butterfly must be
text:
The aptitude to fly,
Meadows of majesty concedes
And easy sweeps of sky.

title: So I must baffle at the hint
text:
And cipher at the sign,
And make much blunder, if at last
I take the clew divine.

title: Poem 412
text: VII.

title: Poem 413
text: SETTING SAIL.

title: Exultation is the going
text:
Of an inland soul to sea, –
Past the houses, past the headlands,
Into deep eternity!

title: Bred as we, among the mountains,
text:
Can the sailor understand
The divine intoxication
Of the first league out from land?

title: Poem 416
text: VIII.

title: Look back on time with kindly eyes,
Poem 418
text: IX.

title: A train went through a burial gate,
And then adjusted his little notes,
text:
And bowed and sang again.
Doubtless, he thought it meet of him
To say good–by to men.

title: Poem 421
text: X.

title: I died for beauty, but was scarce
text:
Adjusted in the tomb,
When one who died for truth was lain
In an adjoining room.

title: He questioned softly why I failed?
And so, as kinsmen met a night,
text:
We talked between the rooms,
Until the moss had reached our lips,
And covered up our names.

title: Poem 425
text: XI.

title: Poem 426
text: "TROUBLED ABOUT MANY THINGS."

title: How many times these low feet staggered,
Stroke the cool forehead, hot so often,
Buzz the dull flies on the chamber window;
Poem 430
text: XII.

title: Poem 431
text: REAL.

title: I like a look of agony,
The eyes glaze once, and that is death.
text:
Impossible to feign
The beads upon the forehead
By homely anguish strung.

title: Poem 434
text: XIII.

title: Poem 435
text: THE FUNERAL.

title: That short, potential stir
text:
That each can make but once,
That bustle so illustrious
'T is almost consequence,

title: Is the eclat of death.
text:
Oh, thou unknown renown
That not a beggar would accept,
Had he the power to spurn!

title: Poem 438
text: XIV.

title: I went to thank her,
text:
But she slept;
Her bed a funnelled stone,
With nosegays at the head and foot,
That travellers had thrown,

title: Who went to thank her;
text:
But she slept.
'T was short to cross the sea
To look upon her like, alive,
But turning back 't was slow.

title: Poem 441
text: XV.

title: I've seen a dying eye
text:
Run round and round a room
In search of something, as it seemed,
Then cloudier become;
And then, obscure with fog,
And then be soldered down,
Without disclosing what it be,
'T were blessed to have seen.

title: Poem 443
text: XVI.

title: Poem 444
text: REFUGE.

title: The clouds their backs together laid,
text:
The north begun to push,
The forests galloped till they fell,
The lightning skipped like mice;
The thunder crumbled like a stuff –
How good to be safe in tombs,
Where nature's temper cannot reach,
Nor vengeance ever comes!

title: Poem 446
text: XVII.

title: I never saw a moor,
I never spoke with God,
Poem 449
text: XVIII.

title: Poem 450
text: PLAYMATES.

title: God permits industrious angels
text:
Afternoons to play.
I met one, – forgot my school–mates,
All, for him, straightway.

title: God calls home the angels promptly
Poem 453
text: XIX.

title: To know just how he suffered would be dear;
text:
To know if any human eyes were near
To whom he could intrust his wavering gaze,
Until it settled firm on Paradise.

title: To know if he was patient, part content,
What was his furthest mind, of home, or God,
text:
Or what the distant say
At news that he ceased human nature
On such a day?

title: And wishes, had he any?
text:
Just his sigh, accented,
Had been legible to me.
And was he confident until
Ill fluttered out in everlasting well?

title: And if he spoke, what name was best,
text:
What first,
What one broke off with
At the drowsiest?

title: Was he afraid, or tranquil?
text:
Might he know
How conscious consciousness could grow,
Till love that was, and love too blest to be,
Meet – and the junction be Eternity?

title: Poem 460
text: XX.

title: The last night that she lived,
text:
It was a common night,
Except the dying; this to us
Made nature different.

title: We noticed smallest things, –
text:
Things overlooked before,
By this great light upon our minds
Italicized, as 't were.

title: That others could exist
text:
While she must finish quite,
A jealousy for her arose
So nearly infinite.

title: We waited while she passed;
text:
It was a narrow time,
Too jostled were our souls to speak,
At length the notice came.

title: She mentioned, and forgot;
text:
Then lightly as a reed
Bent to the water, shivered scarce,
Consented, and was dead.

title: And we, we placed the hair,
Poem 467
text: XXI.

title: Poem 468
text: THE FIRST LESSON.

title: Not in this world to see his face
text:
Sounds long, until I read the place
Where this is said to be
But just the primer to a life
Unopened, rare, upon the shelf,
Clasped yet to him and me.

title: And yet, my primer suits me so
text:
I would not choose a book to know
Than that, be sweeter wise;
Might some one else so learned be,
And leave me just my A B C,
Himself could have the skies.

title: Poem 471
text: XXII.

title: The bustle in a house
text:
The morning after death
Is solemnest of industries
Enacted upon earth, –

title: The sweeping up the heart,
text:
And putting love away
We shall not want to use again
Until eternity.

title: Poem 474
text: XXIII.

title: I reason, earth is short,
I reason, we could die:
I reason that in heaven
Poem 478
text: XXIV.

title: Afraid? Of whom am I afraid?
text:
Not death; for who is he?
The porter of my father's lodge
As much abasheth me.

title: Of life? 'T were odd I fear a thing
text:
That comprehendeth me
In one or more existences
At Deity's decree.

title: Of resurrection? Is the east
text:
Afraid to trust the morn
With her fastidious forehead?
As soon impeach my crown!

title: Poem 482
text: XXV.

title: Poem 483
text: DYING.

title: The sun kept setting, setting still;
text:
No hue of afternoon
Upon the village I perceived, –
From house to house 't was noon.

title: The dusk kept dropping, dropping still;
text:
No dew upon the grass,
But only on my forehead stopped,
And wandered in my face.

title: My feet kept drowsing, drowsing still,
How well I knew the light before!
text:
I could not see it now.
'T is dying, I am doing; but
I'm not afraid to know.

title: Poem 488
text: XXVI.

title: Two swimmers wrestled on the spar
text:
Until the morning sun,
When one turned smiling to the land.
O God, the other one!

title: The stray ships passing spied a face
text:
Upon the waters borne,
With eyes in death still begging raised,
And hands beseeching thrown.

title: Poem 491
text: XXVII.

title: Poem 492
text: THE CHARIOT.

title: Because I could not stop for Death,
We slowly drove, he knew no haste,
text:
And I had put away
My labor, and my leisure too,
For his civility.

title: We passed the school where children played,
We paused before a house that seemed
Since then 't is centuries; but each
text:
Feels shorter than the day
I first surmised the horses' heads
Were toward eternity.

title: Poem 498
text: XXVIII.

title: She went as quiet as the dew
text:
From a familiar flower.
Not like the dew did she return
At the accustomed hour!

title: She dropt as softly as a star
Poem 501
text: XXIX.

title: Poem 502
text: RESURGAM.

title: At last to be identified!
text:
At last, the lamps upon thy side,
The rest of life to see!
Past midnight, past the morning star!
Past sunrise! Ah! what leagues there are
Between our feet and day!

title: Poem 504
text: XXX.

title: Except to heaven, she is nought;
Except for winds, provincial;
text:
Except by butterflies,
Unnoticed as a single dew
That on the acre lies.

title: The smallest housewife in the grass,
text:
Yet take her from the lawn,
And somebody has lost the face
That made existence home!

title: Poem 508
text: XXXI.

title: Death is a dialogue between
text:
The spirit and the dust.
"Dissolve," says Death. The Spirit, "Sir,
I have another trust."

title: Death doubts it, argues from the ground.
text:
The Spirit turns away,
Just laying off, for evidence,
An overcoat of clay.

title: Poem 511
text: XXXII.

title: It was too late for man,
How excellent the heaven,
Poem 514
text: XXXIII.

title: Poem 515
text: ALONG THE POTOMAC.

title: When I was small, a woman died.
text:
To–day her only boy
Went up from the Potomac,
His face all victory,

title: To look at her; how slowly
text:
The seasons must have turned
Till bullets clipt an angle,
And he passed quickly round!

title: If pride shall be in Paradise
But proud in apparition,
text:
That woman and her boy
Pass back and forth before my brain,
As ever in the sky.

title: Poem 520
text: XXXIV.

title: The daisy follows soft the sun,
text:
And when his golden walk is done,
Sits shyly at his feet.
He, waking, finds the flower near.
"Wherefore, marauder, art thou here?"
"Because, sir, love is sweet!"

title: We are the flower, Thou the sun!
text:
Forgive us, if as days decline,
We nearer steal to Thee, –
Enamoured of the parting west,
The peace, the flight, the amethyst,
Night's possibility!

title: Poem 523
text: XXXV.

title: Poem 524
text: EMANCIPATION.

title: No rack can torture me,
text:
My soul's at liberty
Behind this mortal bone
There knits a bolder one

title: You cannot prick with saw,
The eagle of his nest
text:
No easier divest
And gain the sky,
Than mayest thou,

title: Except thyself may be
Poem 529
text: XXXVI.

title: Poem 530
text: LOST.

title: I lost a world the other day.
text:
Has anybody found?
You'll know it by the row of stars
Around its forehead bound.

title: A rich man might not notice it;
text:
Yet to my frugal eye
Of more esteem than ducats.
Oh, find it, sir, for me!

title: Poem 533
text: XXXVII.

title: If I shouldn't be alive
text:
When the robins come,
Give the one in red cravat
A memorial crumb.

title: If I couldn't thank you,
text:
Being just asleep,
You will know I'm trying
With my granite lip!

title: Poem 536
text: XXXVIII.

title: Sleep is supposed to be,
text:
By souls of sanity,
The shutting of the eye.

title: Sleep is the station grand
text:
Down which on either hand
The hosts of witness stand!

title: Morn is supposed to be,
text:
By people of degree,
The breaking of the day.

title: Morning has not occurred!
text:
That shall aurora be
East of eternity;

title: One with the banner gay,
text:
One in the red array, –
That is the break of day.

title: Poem 542
text: XXXIX.

title: I shall know why, when time is over,
He will tell me what Peter promised,
text:
And I, for wonder at his woe,
I shall forget the drop of anguish
That scalds me now, that scalds me now.

title: Poem 545
text: XL.

title: I never lost as much but twice,
Angels, twice descending,
text:
Reimbursed my store.
Burglar, banker, father,
I am poor once more!

title: POEMS
text:
by EMILY DICKINSON
Second Series

title: Poem 549
text: Edited by two of her friends

title: Poem 550
text: MABEL LOOMIS TODD and T.W. HIGGINSON

title: Poem 551
text: PREFACE

title: Poem 552
text: The eagerness with which the first volume of Emily Dickinson's poems has been read shows very clearly that all our alleged modern artificiality does not prevent a prompt appreciation of the qualities of directness and simplicity in approaching the greatest themes,–life and love and death. That "irresistible needle–touch," as one of her best critics has called it, piercing at once the very core of a thought, has found a response as wide and sympathetic as it has been unexpected even to those who knew best her compelling power. This second volume, while open to the same criticism as to form with its predecessor, shows also the same shining beauties.

title: Poem 553
text: Although Emily Dickinson had been in the habit of sending occasional poems to friends and correspondents, the full extent of her writing was by no means imagined by them. Her friend "H.H." must at least have suspected it, for in a letter dated 5th September, 1884, she wrote:–

title: Poem 554
text: MY DEAR FRIEND,– What portfolios full of verses you must have! It is a cruel wrong to your "day and generation" that you will not give them light.

title: Poem 555
text: If such a thing should happen as that I should outlive you, I wish you would make me your literary legatee and executor. Surely after you are what is called "dead" you will be willing that the poor ghosts you have left behind should be cheered and pleased by your verses, will you not? You ought to be. I do not think we have a right to withhold from the world a word or a thought any more than a deed which might help a single soul. . . .

title: Poem 556
text: Truly yours,

title: Poem 557
text: HELEN JACKSON.

title: Poem 558
text: The "portfolios" were found, shortly after Emily Dickinson's death, by her sister and only surviving housemate. Most of the poems had been carefully copied on sheets of note–paper, and tied in little fascicules, each of six or eight sheets. While many of them bear evidence of having been thrown off at white heat, still more had received thoughtful revision. There is the frequent addition of rather perplexing foot–notes, affording large choice of words and phrases. And in the copies which she sent to friends, sometimes one form, sometimes another, is found to have been used. Without important exception, her friends have generously placed at the disposal of the Editors any poems they had received from her; and these have given the obvious advantage of comparison among several renderings of the same verse.

title: Poem 559
text: To what further rigorous pruning her verses would have been subjected had she published them herself, we cannot know. They should be regarded in many cases as merely the first strong and suggestive sketches of an artist, intended to be embodied at some time in the finished picture.

title: Poem 560
text: Emily Dickinson appears to have written her first poems in the winter of 1862. In a letter to one of the present Editors the April following, she says, "I made no verse, but one or two, until this winter."

title: Poem 561
text: The handwriting was at first somewhat like the delicate, running Italian hand of our elder gentlewomen; but as she advanced in breadth of thought, it grew bolder and more abrupt, until in her latest years each letter stood distinct and separate from its fellows. In most of her poems, particularly the later ones, everything by way of punctuation was discarded, except numerous dashes; and all important words began with capitals. The effect of a page of her more recent manuscript is exceedingly quaint and strong. The fac–simile given in the present volume is from one of the earlier transition periods. Although there is nowhere a date, the handwriting makes it possible to arrange the poems with general chronologic accuracy.

title: Poem 562
text: As a rule, the verses were without titles; but "A Country Burial," "A Thunder–Storm," "The Humming–Bird," and a few others were named by their author, frequently at the end,–sometimes only in the accompanying note, if sent to a friend.

title: Poem 563
text: The variation of readings, with the fact that she often wrote in pencil and not always clearly, have at times thrown a good deal of responsibility upon her Editors. But all interference not absolutely inevitable has been avoided. The very roughness of her rendering is part of herself, and not lightly to be touched; for it seems in many cases that she intentionally avoided the smoother and more usual rhymes.

title: Poem 564
text: Like impressionist pictures, or Wagner's rugged music, the very absence of conventional form challenges attention. In Emily Dickinson's exacting hands, the especial, intrinsic fitness of a particular order of words might not be sacrificed to anything virtually extrinsic; and her verses all show a strange cadence of inner rhythmical music. Lines are always daringly constructed, and the "thought–rhyme" appears frequently,–appealing, indeed, to an unrecognized sense more elusive than hearing.

title: Poem 565
text: Emily Dickinson scrutinized everything with clear–eyed frankness. Every subject was proper ground for legitimate study, even the sombre facts of death and burial, and the unknown life beyond. She touches these themes sometimes lightly, sometimes almost humorously, more often with weird and peculiar power; but she is never by any chance frivolous or trivial. And while, as one critic has said, she may exhibit toward God "an Emersonian self–possession," it was because she looked upon all life with a candor as unprejudiced as it is rare.

title: Poem 566
text: She had tried society and the world, and found them lacking. She was not an invalid, and she lived in seclusion from no love–disappointment. Her life was the normal blossoming of a nature introspective to a high degree, whose best thought could not exist in pretence.

title: Poem 567
text: Storm, wind, the wild March sky, sunsets and dawns; the birds and bees, butterflies and flowers of her garden, with a few trusted human friends, were sufficient companionship. The coming of the first robin was a jubilee beyond crowning of monarch or birthday of pope; the first red leaf hurrying through "the altered air," an epoch. Immortality was close about her; and while never morbid or melancholy, she lived in its presence.

title: Poem 568
text: MABEL LOOMIS TODD.

title: Poem 569
text: AMHERST, MASSACHUSETTS,

title: Poem 570
text: August, 1891.

title: My nosegays are for captives;
text:
Dim, long–expectant eyes,
Fingers denied the plucking,
Patient till paradise,

title: To such, if they should whisper
text:
Of morning and the moor,
They bear no other errand,
And I, no other prayer.

title: Poem 573
text: I. LIFE.

title: Poem 574
text: I.

title: I'm nobody! Who are you?
text:
Are you nobody, too?
Then there 's a pair of us – don't tell!
They 'd banish us, you know.

title: How dreary to be somebody!
text:
How public, like a frog
To tell your name the livelong day
To an admiring bog!

title: Poem 577
text: II.

title: I bring an unaccustomed wine
text:
To lips long parching, next to mine,
And summon them to drink.

title: Crackling with fever, they essay;
text:
I turn my brimming eyes away,
And come next hour to look.

title: The hands still hug the tardy glass;
text:
The lips I would have cooled, alas!
Are so superfluous cold,

title: I would as soon attempt to warm
text:
The bosoms where the frost has lain
Ages beneath the mould.

title: Some other thirsty there may be
text:
To whom this would have pointed me
Had it remained to speak.

title: And so I always bear the cup
text:
If, haply, mine may be the drop
Some pilgrim thirst to slake, –

title: If, haply, any say to me,
text:
"Unto the little, unto me,"
When I at last awake.

title: Poem 585
text: III.

title: The nearest dream recedes, unrealized.
text:
The heaven we chase
Like the June bee
Before the school–boy
Invites the race;
Stoops to an easy clover –
Dips – evades – teases – deploys;
Then to the royal clouds
Lifts his light pinnace
Heedless of the boy
Staring, bewildered, at the mocking sky.

title: Homesick for steadfast honey,
text:
Ah! the bee flies not
That brews that rare variety.

title: Poem 588
text: IV.

title: We play at paste,
text:
Till qualified for pearl,
Then drop the paste,
And deem ourself a fool.
The shapes, though, were similar,
And our new hands
Learned gem–tactics
Practising sands.

title: Poem 590
text: V.

title: I found the phrase to every thought
To races nurtured in the dark; –
text:
How would your own begin?
Can blaze be done in cochineal,
Or noon in mazarin?

title: Poem 593
text: VI.

title: Poem 594
text: HOPE.

title: Hope is the thing with feathers
text:
That perches in the soul,
And sings the tune without the words,
And never stops at all,

title: And sweetest in the gale is heard;
text:
And sore must be the storm
That could abash the little bird
That kept so many warm.

title: I 've heard it in the chillest land,
Poem 598
text: VII.

title: Poem 599
text: THE WHITE HEAT.

title: Dare you see a soul at the white heat?
Has sated flame's conditions,
text:
Its quivering substance plays
Without a color but the light
Of unanointed blaze.

title: Least village boasts its blacksmith,
text:
Whose anvil's even din
Stands symbol for the finer forge
That soundless tugs within,

title: Refining these impatient ores
text:
With hammer and with blaze,
Until the designated light
Repudiate the forge.

title: Poem 604
text: VIII.

title: Poem 605
text: TRIUMPHANT.

title: Who never lost, are unprepared
Who never climbed the weary league –
text:
Can such a foot explore
The purple territories
On Pizarro's shore?

title: How many legions overcome?
text:
The emperor will say.
How many colors taken
On Revolution Day?

title: How many bullets bearest?
text:
The royal scar hast thou?
Angels, write "Promoted"
On this soldier's brow!

title: Poem 610
text: IX.

title: Poem 611
text: THE TEST.

title: I can wade grief,
text:
Whole pools of it, –
I 'm used to that.
But the least push of joy
Breaks up my feet,
And I tip – drunken.
Let no pebble smile,
'T was the new liquor, –
That was all!

title: Power is only pain,
text:
Stranded, through discipline,
Till weights will hang.
Give balm to giants,
And they 'll wilt, like men.
Give Himmaleh, –
They 'll carry him!

title: Poem 614
text: X.

title: Poem 615
text: ESCAPE.

title: I never hear the word "escape"
text:
Without a quicker blood,
A sudden expectation,
A flying attitude.

title: I never hear of prisons broad
text:
By soldiers battered down,
But I tug childish at my bars, –
Only to fail again!

title: Poem 618
text: XI.

title: Poem 619
text: COMPENSATION.

title: For each ecstatic instant
text:
We must an anguish pay
In keen and quivering ratio
To the ecstasy.

title: For each beloved hour
text:
Sharp pittances of years,
Bitter contested farthings
And coffers heaped with tears.

title: Poem 622
text: XII.

title: Poem 623
text: THE MARTYRS.

title: Through the straight pass of suffering
text:
The martyrs even trod,
Their feet upon temptation,
Their faces upon God.

title: A stately, shriven company;
text:
Convulsion playing round,
Harmless as streaks of meteor
Upon a planet's bound.

title: Their faith the everlasting troth;
Poem 627
text: XIII.

title: Poem 628
text: A PRAYER.

title: I meant to have but modest needs,
But since the last included both,
text:
It would suffice my prayer
But just for one to stipulate,
And grace would grant the pair.

title: And so, upon this wise I prayed, –
text:
Great Spirit, give to me
A heaven not so large as yours,
But large enough for me.

title: A smile suffused Jehovah's face;
I left the place with all my might, –
That one so honest be extant
text:
As take the tale for true
That "Whatsoever you shall ask,
Itself be given you."

title: But I, grown shrewder, scan the skies
text:
With a suspicious air, –
As children, swindled for the first,
All swindlers be, infer.

title: Poem 636
text: XIV.

title: The thought beneath so slight a film
text:
Is more distinctly seen, –
As laces just reveal the surge,
Or mists the Apennine.

title: Poem 638
text: XV.

title: The soul unto itself
text:
Is an imperial friend, –
Or the most agonizing spy
An enemy could send.

title: Secure against its own,
Poem 641
text: XVI.

title: Surgeons must be very careful
text:
When they take the knife!
Underneath their fine incisions
Stirs the culprit, – Life!

title: Poem 643
text: XVII.

title: Poem 644
text: THE RAILWAY TRAIN.

title: I like to see it lap the miles,
Around a pile of mountains,
To fit its sides, and crawl between,
And neigh like Boanerges;
text:
Then, punctual as a star,
Stop – docile and omnipotent –
At its own stable door.

title: Poem 649
text: XVIII.

title: Poem 650
text: THE SHOW.

title: The show is not the show,
text:
But they that go.
Menagerie to me
My neighbor be.
Fair play –
Both went to see.

title: Poem 652
text: XIX.

title: Delight becomes pictorial
text:
When viewed through pain, –
More fair, because impossible
That any gain.

title: The mountain at a given distance
Poem 655
text: XX.

title: A thought went up my mind to–day
text:
That I have had before,
But did not finish, – some way back,
I could not fix the year,

title: Nor where it went, nor why it came
text:
The second time to me,
Nor definitely what it was,
Have I the art to say.

title: But somewhere in my soul, I know
Poem 659
text: XXI.

title: Is Heaven a physician?
text:
They say that He can heal,
But medicine posthumous
Is unavailable.

title: Is Heaven an exchequer?
Poem 662
text: XXII.

title: Poem 663
text: THE RETURN.

title: Though I get home how late, how late!
text:
So I get home, 't will compensate.
Better will be the ecstasy
That they have done expecting me,
When, night descending, dumb and dark,
They hear my unexpected knock.
Transporting must the moment be,
Brewed from decades of agony!

title: To think just how the fire will burn,
text:
Just how long–cheated eyes will turn
To wonder what myself will say,
And what itself will say to me,
Beguiles the centuries of way!

title: Poem 666
text: XXIII.

title: A poor torn heart, a tattered heart,
text:
That sat it down to rest,
Nor noticed that the ebbing day
Flowed silver to the west,
Nor noticed night did soft descend
Nor constellation burn,
Intent upon the vision
Of latitudes unknown.

title: The angels, happening that way,
text:
This dusty heart espied;
Tenderly took it up from toil
And carried it to God.
There, – sandals for the barefoot;
There, – gathered from the gales,
Do the blue havens by the hand
Lead the wandering sails.

title: Poem 669
text: XXIV.

title: Poem 670
text: TOO MUCH.

title: I should have been too glad, I see,
text:
Too lifted for the scant degree
Of life's penurious round;
My little circuit would have shamed
This new circumference, have blamed
The homelier time behind.

title: I should have been too saved, I see,
text:
Too rescued; fear too dim to me
That I could spell the prayer
I knew so perfect yesterday, –
That scalding one, "Sabachthani,"
Recited fluent here.

title: Earth would have been too much, I see,
Defeat whets victory, they say;
text:
The reefs in old Gethsemane
Endear the shore beyond.
'T is beggars banquets best define;
'T is thirsting vitalizes wine, –
Faith faints to understand.

title: Poem 675
text: XXV.

title: Poem 676
text: SHIPWRECK.

title: It tossed and tossed, –
text:
A little brig I knew, –
O'ertook by blast,
It spun and spun,
And groped delirious, for morn.

title: It slipped and slipped,
Ah, brig, good–night
Poem 680
text: XXVI.

title: Victory comes late,
text:
And is held low to freezing lips
Too rapt with frost
To take it.
How sweet it would have tasted,
Just a drop!
Was God so economical?
His table 's spread too high for us
Unless we dine on tip–toe.
Crumbs fit such little mouths,
Cherries suit robins;
The eagle's golden breakfast
Strangles them.
God keeps his oath to sparrows,
Who of little love
Know how to starve!

title: Poem 682
text: XXVII.

title: Poem 683
text: ENOUGH.

title: God gave a loaf to every bird,
text:
But just a crumb to me;
I dare not eat it, though I starve, –
My poignant luxury
To own it, touch it, prove the feat
That made the pellet mine, –
Too happy in my sparrow chance
For ampler coveting.

title: It might be famine all around,
text:
I could not miss an ear,
Such plenty smiles upon my board,
My garner shows so fair.
I wonder how the rich may feel, –
An Indiaman – an Earl?
I deem that I with but a crumb
Am sovereign of them all.

title: Poem 686
text: XXVIII.

title: Experiment to me
text:
Is every one I meet.
If it contain a kernel?
The figure of a nut

title: Presents upon a tree,
Poem 689
text: XXIX.

title: Poem 690
text: MY COUNTRY'S WARDROBE.

title: My country need not change her gown,
text:
Her triple suit as sweet
As when 't was cut at Lexington,
And first pronounced "a fit."

title: Great Britain disapproves "the stars;"
text:
Disparagement discreet, –
There 's something in their attitude
That taunts her bayonet.

title: Poem 693
text: XXX.

title: Faith is a fine invention
Poem 695
text: XXXI.

title: Except the heaven had come so near,
But just to hear the grace depart
Poem 698
text: XXXII.

title: Portraits are to daily faces
text:
As an evening west
To a fine, pedantic sunshine
In a satin vest.

title: Poem 700
text: XXXIII.

title: Poem 701
text: THE DUEL.

title: I took my power in my hand.
I aimed my pebble, but myself
text:
Was all the one that fell.
Was it Goliath was too large,
Or only I too small?

title: Poem 704
text: XXXIV.

title: A shady friend for torrid days
text:
Is easier to find
Than one of higher temperature
For frigid hour of mind.

title: The vane a little to the east
Who is to blame? The weaver?
text:
Ah! the bewildering thread!
The tapestries of paradise
So notelessly are made!

title: Poem 708
text: XXXV.

title: Poem 709
text: THE GOAL.

title: Each life converges to some centre
Admitted scarcely to itself, it may be,
text:
Too fair
For credibility's temerity
To dare.

title: Adored with caution, as a brittle heaven,
text:
To reach
Were hopeless as the rainbow's raiment
To touch,

title: Yet persevered toward, surer for the distance;
text:
How high
Unto the saints' slow diligence
The sky!

title: Ungained, it may be, by a life's low venture,
text:
But then,
Eternity enables the endeavoring
Again.

title: Poem 715
text: XXXVI.

title: Poem 716
text: SIGHT.

title: Before I got my eye put out,
text:
I liked as well to see
As other creatures that have eyes,
And know no other way.

title: But were it told to me, to–day,
text:
That I might have the sky
For mine, I tell you that my heart
Would split, for size of me.

title: The meadows mine, the mountains mine, –
text:
All forests, stintless stars,
As much of noon as I could take
Between my finite eyes.

title: The motions of the dipping birds,
text:
The lightning's jointed road,
For mine to look at when I liked, –
The news would strike me dead!

title: So safer, guess, with just my soul
text:
Upon the window–pane
Where other creatures put their eyes,
Incautious of the sun.

title: Poem 722
text: XXXVII.

title: Talk with prudence to a beggar
text:
Of 'Potosi' and the mines!
Reverently to the hungry
Of your viands and your wines!

title: Cautious, hint to any captive
text:
You have passed enfranchised feet!
Anecdotes of air in dungeons
Have sometimes proved deadly sweet!

title: Poem 725
text: XXXVIII.

title: Poem 726
text: THE PREACHER.

title: He preached upon "breadth" till it argued him narrow, –
Simplicity fled from his counterfeit presence
text:
As gold the pyrites would shun.
What confusion would cover the innocent Jesus
To meet so enabled a man!

title: Poem 729
text: XXXIX.

title: Good night! which put the candle out?
text:
A jealous zephyr, not a doubt.
Ah! friend, you little knew
How long at that celestial wick
The angels labored diligent;
Extinguished, now, for you!

title: It might have been the lighthouse spark
text:
Some sailor, rowing in the dark,
Had importuned to see!
It might have been the waning lamp
That lit the drummer from the camp
To purer reveille!

title: Poem 732
text: XL.

title: When I hoped I feared,
Poem 734
text: XLI.

title: Poem 735
text: DEED.

title: A deed knocks first at thought,
text:
And then it knocks at will.
That is the manufacturing spot,
And will at home and well.

title: It then goes out an act,
text:
Or is entombed so still
That only to the ear of God
Its doom is audible.

title: Poem 738
text: XLII.

title: Poem 739
text: TIME'S LESSON.

title: Mine enemy is growing old, –
Let him be quick, the viand flits,
Poem 742
text: XLIII.

title: Poem 743
text: REMORSE.

title: Remorse is memory awake,
text:
Her companies astir, –
A presence of departed acts
At window and at door.

title: It's past set down before the soul,
text:
And lighted with a match,
Perusal to facilitate
Of its condensed despatch.

title: Remorse is cureless, – the disease
Poem 747
text: XLIV.

title: Poem 748
text: THE SHELTER.

title: The body grows outside, –
text:
The more convenient way, –
That if the spirit like to hide,
Its temple stands alway

title: Ajar, secure, inviting;
text:
It never did betray
The soul that asked its shelter
In timid honesty.

title: Poem 751
text: XLV.

title: Undue significance a starving man attaches
text:
To food
Far off; he sighs, and therefore hopeless,
And therefore good.

title: Partaken, it relieves indeed, but proves us
text:
That spices fly
In the receipt. It was the distance
Was savory.

title: Poem 754
text: XLVI.

title: Heart not so heavy as mine,
text:
Wending late home,
As it passed my window
Whistled itself a tune, –

title: A careless snatch, a ballad,
It was as if a bobolink,
text:
Sauntering this way,
Carolled and mused and carolled,
Then bubbled slow away.

title: It was as if a chirping brook
text:
Upon a toilsome way
Set bleeding feet to minuets
Without the knowing why.

title: To–morrow, night will come again,
text:
Weary, perhaps, and sore.
Ah, bugle, by my window,
I pray you stroll once more!

title: Poem 760
text: XLVII.

title: I many times thought peace had come,
And struggle slacker, but to prove,
text:
As hopelessly as I,
How many the fictitious shores
Before the harbor lie.

title: Poem 763
text: XLVIII.

title: Unto my books so good to turn
As flavors cheer retarded guests
text:
With banquetings to be,
So spices stimulate the time
Till my small library.

title: It may be wilderness without,
text:
Far feet of failing men,
But holiday excludes the night,
And it is bells within.

title: I thank these kinsmen of the shelf;
text:
Their countenances bland
Enamour in prospective,
And satisfy, obtained.

title: Poem 768
text: XLIX.

title: This merit hath the worst, –
text:
It cannot be again.
When Fate hath taunted last
And thrown her furthest stone,

title: The maimed may pause and breathe,
text:
And glance securely round.
The deer invites no longer
Than it eludes the hound.

title: Poem 771
text: L.

title: Poem 772
text: HUNGER.

title: I had been hungry all the years;
'T was this on tables I had seen,
text:
When turning, hungry, lone,
I looked in windows, for the wealth
I could not hope to own.

title: I did not know the ample bread,
text:
'T was so unlike the crumb
The birds and I had often shared
In Nature's dining–room.

title: The plenty hurt me, 't was so new, –
text:
Myself felt ill and odd,
As berry of a mountain bush
Transplanted to the road.

title: Nor was I hungry; so I found
text:
That hunger was a way
Of persons outside windows,
The entering takes away.

title: Poem 778
text: LI.

title: I gained it so,
text:
By climbing slow,
By catching at the twigs that grow
Between the bliss and me.
It hung so high,
As well the sky
Attempt by strategy.

title: I said I gained it, –
text:
This was all.
Look, how I clutch it,
Lest it fall,
And I a pauper go;
Unfitted by an instant's grace
For the contented beggar's face
I wore an hour ago.

title: Poem 781
text: LII.

title: To learn the transport by the pain,
To stay the homesick, homesick feet
text:
Upon a foreign shore
Haunted by native lands, the while,
And blue, beloved air –

title: This is the sovereign anguish,
text:
This, the signal woe!
These are the patient laureates
Whose voices, trained below,

title: Ascend in ceaseless carol,
text:
Inaudible, indeed,
To us, the duller scholars
Of the mysterious bard!

title: Poem 786
text: LIII.

title: Poem 787
text: RETURNING.

title: I years had been from home,
text:
And now, before the door,
I dared not open, lest a face
I never saw before

title: Stare vacant into mine
text:
And ask my business there.
My business, – just a life I left,
Was such still dwelling there?

title: I fumbled at my nerve,
I laughed a wooden laugh
text:
That I could fear a door,
Who danger and the dead had faced,
But never quaked before.

title: I fitted to the latch
text:
My hand, with trembling care,
Lest back the awful door should spring,
And leave me standing there.

title: I moved my fingers off
text:
As cautiously as glass,
And held my ears, and like a thief
Fled gasping from the house.

title: Poem 794
text: LIV.

title: Poem 795
text: PRAYER.

title: Prayer is the little implement
text:
Through which men reach
Where presence is denied them.
They fling their speech

title: By means of it in God's ear;
text:
If then He hear,
This sums the apparatus
Comprised in prayer.

title: Poem 798
text: LV.

title: I know that he exists
text:
Somewhere, in silence.
He has hid his rare life
From our gross eyes.

title: 'T is an instant's play,
text:
'T is a fond ambush,
Just to make bliss
Earn her own surprise!

title: But should the play
text:
Prove piercing earnest,
Should the glee glaze
In death's stiff stare,

title: Would not the fun
text:
Look too expensive?
Would not the jest
Have crawled too far?

title: Poem 803
text: LVI.

title: Poem 804
text: MELODIES UNHEARD.

title: Musicians wrestle everywhere:
text:
All day, among the crowded air,
I hear the silver strife;
And – waking long before the dawn –
Such transport breaks upon the town
I think it that "new life!"

title: It is not bird, it has no nest;
text:
Nor band, in brass and scarlet dressed,
Nor tambourine, nor man;
It is not hymn from pulpit read, –
The morning stars the treble led
On time's first afternoon!

title: Some say it is the spheres at play!
text:
Some say that bright majority
Of vanished dames and men!
Some think it service in the place
Where we, with late, celestial face,
Please God, shall ascertain!

title: Poem 808
text: LVII.

title: Poem 809
text: CALLED BACK.

title: Just lost when I was saved!
text:
Just felt the world go by!
Just girt me for the onset with eternity,
When breath blew back,
And on the other side
I heard recede the disappointed tide!

title: Therefore, as one returned, I feel,
text:
Odd secrets of the line to tell!
Some sailor, skirting foreign shores,
Some pale reporter from the awful doors
Before the seal!

title: Next time, to stay!
text:
Next time, the things to see
By ear unheard,
Unscrutinized by eye.

title: Next time, to tarry,
text:
While the ages steal, –
Slow tramp the centuries,
And the cycles wheel.

title: Poem 814
text: II. LOVE.

title: Poem 815
text: I.

title: Poem 816
text: CHOICE.

title: Of all the souls that stand create
When that which is and that which was
When figures show their royal front
text:
And mists are carved away, –
Behold the atom I preferred
To all the lists of clay!

title: Poem 820
text: II.

title: I have no life but this,
Nor tie to earths to come,
text:
Nor action new,
Except through this extent,
The realm of you.

title: Poem 823
text: III.

title: Your riches taught me poverty.
text:
Myself a millionnaire
In little wealths, – as girls could boast, –
Till broad as Buenos Ayre,

title: You drifted your dominions
Of mines I little know, myself,
So much that, did I meet the queen,
text:
Her glory I should know:
But this must be a different wealth,
To miss it beggars so.

title: I 'm sure 't is India all day
text:
To those who look on you
Without a stint, without a blame, –
Might I but be the Jew!

title: I 'm sure it is Golconda,
text:
Beyond my power to deem, –
To have a smile for mine each day,
How better than a gem!

title: At least, it solaces to know
text:
That there exists a gold,
Although I prove it just in time
Its distance to behold!

title: It 's far, far treasure to surmise,
text:
And estimate the pearl
That slipped my simple fingers through
While just a girl at school!

title: Poem 832
text: IV.

title: Poem 833
text: THE CONTRACT.

title: I gave myself to him,
text:
And took himself for pay.
The solemn contract of a life
Was ratified this way.

title: The wealth might disappoint,
text:
Myself a poorer prove
Than this great purchaser suspect,
The daily own of Love

title: Depreciate the vision;
text:
But, till the merchant buy,
Still fable, in the isles of spice,
The subtle cargoes lie.

title: At least, 't is mutual risk, –
Poem 838
text: V.

title: Poem 839
text: THE LETTER.

title: "Going to him! Happy letter! Tell him –
text:
Tell him the page I didn't write;
Tell him I only said the syntax,
And left the verb and the pronoun out.
Tell him just how the fingers hurried,
Then how they waded, slow, slow, slow;
And then you wished you had eyes in your pages,
So you could see what moved them so.

title: "Tell him it wasn't a practised writer,
text:
You guessed, from the way the sentence toiled;
You could hear the bodice tug, behind you,
As if it held but the might of a child;
You almost pitied it, you, it worked so.
Tell him – No, you may quibble there,
For it would split his heart to know it,
And then you and I were silenter.

title: "Tell him night finished before we finished,
text:
And the old clock kept neighing 'day!'
And you got sleepy and begged to be ended –
What could it hinder so, to say?
Tell him just how she sealed you, cautious,
But if he ask where you are hid
Until to–morrow, – happy letter!
Gesture, coquette, and shake your head!"

title: Poem 843
text: VI.

title: The way I read a letter 's this:
text:
'T is first I lock the door,
And push it with my fingers next,
For transport it be sure.

title: And then I go the furthest off
Then, glancing narrow at the wall,
text:
And narrow at the floor,
For firm conviction of a mouse
Not exorcised before,

title: Peruse how infinite I am
text:
To – no one that you know!
And sigh for lack of heaven, – but not
The heaven the creeds bestow.

title: Poem 848
text: VII.

title: Wild nights! Wild nights!
text:
Were I with thee,
Wild nights should be
Our luxury!

title: Futile the winds
text:
To a heart in port, –
Done with the compass,
Done with the chart.

title: Rowing in Eden!
text:
Ah! the sea!
Might I but moor
To–night in thee!

title: Poem 852
text: VIII.

title: Poem 853
text: AT HOME.

title: The night was wide, and furnished scant
text:
With but a single star,
That often as a cloud it met
Blew out itself for fear.

title: The wind pursued the little bush,
text:
And drove away the leaves
November left; then clambered up
And fretted in the eaves.

title: No squirrel went abroad;
text:
A dog's belated feet
Like intermittent plush were heard
Adown the empty street.

title: To feel if blinds be fast,
text:
And closer to the fire
Her little rocking–chair to draw,
And shiver for the poor,

title: The housewife's gentle task.
text:
"How pleasanter," said she
Unto the sofa opposite,
"The sleet than May – no thee!"

title: Poem 859
text: IX.

title: Poem 860
text: POSSESSION.

title: Did the harebell loose her girdle
text:
To the lover bee,
Would the bee the harebell hallow
Much as formerly?

title: Did the paradise, persuaded,
text:
Yield her moat of pearl,
Would the Eden be an Eden,
Or the earl an earl?

title: Poem 863
text: X.

title: A charm invests a face
text:
Imperfectly beheld, –
The lady dare not lift her veil
For fear it be dispelled.

title: But peers beyond her mesh,
text:
And wishes, and denies, –
Lest interview annul a want
That image satisfies.

title: Poem 866
text: XI.

title: Poem 867
text: THE LOVERS.

title: The rose did caper on her cheek,
text:
Her bodice rose and fell,
Her pretty speech, like drunken men,
Did stagger pitiful.

title: Her fingers fumbled at her work, –
Till opposite I spied a cheek
A vest that, like the bodice, danced
text:
To the immortal tune, –
Till those two troubled little clocks
Ticked softly into one.

title: Poem 872
text: XII.

title: In lands I never saw, they say,
text:
Immortal Alps look down,
Whose bonnets touch the firmament,
Whose sandals touch the town, –

title: Meek at whose everlasting feet
text:
A myriad daisies play.
Which, sir, are you, and which am I,
Upon an August day?

title: Poem 875
text: XIII.

title: The moon is distant from the sea,
text:
And yet with amber hands
She leads him, docile as a boy,
Along appointed sands.

title: He never misses a degree;
text:
Obedient to her eye,
