He comes just so far toward the town,
Just so far goes away.

title: Oh, Signor, thine the amber hand,
text:
And mine the distant sea, –
Obedient to the least command
Thine eyes impose on me.

title: Poem 879
text: XIV.

title: He put the belt around my life, –
text:
I heard the buckle snap,
And turned away, imperial,
My lifetime folding up
Deliberate, as a duke would do
A kingdom's title–deed, –
Henceforth a dedicated sort,
A member of the cloud.

title: Yet not too far to come at call,
text:
And do the little toils
That make the circuit of the rest,
And deal occasional smiles
To lives that stoop to notice mine
And kindly ask it in, –
Whose invitation, knew you not
For whom I must decline?

title: Poem 882
text: XV.

title: Poem 883
text: THE LOST JEWEL.

title: I held a jewel in my fingers
I woke and chid my honest fingers, –
Poem 886
text: XVI.

title: What if I say I shall not wait?
text:
What if I burst the fleshly gate
And pass, escaped, to thee?
What if I file this mortal off,
See where it hurt me, – that 's enough, –
And wade in liberty?

title: They cannot take us any more, –
text:
Dungeons may call, and guns implore;
Unmeaning now, to me,
As laughter was an hour ago,
Or laces, or a travelling show,
Or who died yesterday!

title: Poem 889
text: III. NATURE.

title: Poem 890
text: I.

title: Poem 891
text: MOTHER NATURE.

title: Nature, the gentlest mother,
text:
Impatient of no child,
The feeblest or the waywardest, –
Her admonition mild

title: In forest and the hill
text:
By traveller is heard,
Restraining rampant squirrel
Or too impetuous bird.

title: How fair her conversation,
Her voice among the aisles
text:
Incites the timid prayer
Of the minutest cricket,
The most unworthy flower.

title: When all the children sleep
With infinite affection
text:
And infiniter care,
Her golden finger on her lip,
Wills silence everywhere.

title: Poem 898
text: II.

title: Poem 899
text: OUT OF THE MORNING.

title: Will there really be a morning?
text:
Is there such a thing as day?
Could I see it from the mountains
If I were as tall as they?

title: Has it feet like water–lilies?
text:
Has it feathers like a bird?
Is it brought from famous countries
Of which I have never heard?

title: Oh, some scholar! Oh, some sailor!
text:
Oh, some wise man from the skies!
Please to tell a little pilgrim
Where the place called morning lies!

title: Poem 903
text: III.

title: At half–past three a single bird
text:
Unto a silent sky
Propounded but a single term
Of cautious melody.

title: At half–past four, experiment
text:
Had subjugated test,
And lo! her silver principle
Supplanted all the rest.

title: At half–past seven, element
text:
Nor implement was seen,
And place was where the presence was,
Circumference between.

title: Poem 907
text: IV.

title: Poem 908
text: DAY'S PARLOR.

title: The day came slow, till five o'clock,
text:
Then sprang before the hills
Like hindered rubies, or the light
A sudden musket spills.

title: The purple could not keep the east,
text:
The sunrise shook from fold,
Like breadths of topaz, packed a night,
The lady just unrolled.

title: The happy winds their timbrels took;
text:
The birds, in docile rows,
Arranged themselves around their prince
(The wind is prince of those).

title: The orchard sparkled like a Jew, –
text:
How mighty 't was, to stay
A guest in this stupendous place,
The parlor of the day!

title: Poem 913
text: V.

title: Poem 914
text: THE SUN'S WOOING.

title: The sun just touched the morning;
text:
The morning, happy thing,
Supposed that he had come to dwell,
And life would be all spring.

title: She felt herself supremer, –
Trailed slow along the orchards
text:
His haughty, spangled hems,
Leaving a new necessity, –
The want of diadems!

title: The morning fluttered, staggered,
text:
Felt feebly for her crown, –
Her unanointed forehead
Henceforth her only one.

title: Poem 919
text: VI.

title: Poem 920
text: THE ROBIN.

title: The robin is the one
text:
That interrupts the morn
With hurried, few, express reports
When March is scarcely on.

title: The robin is the one
text:
That overflows the noon
With her cherubic quantity,
An April but begun.

title: The robin is the one
text:
That speechless from her nest
Submits that home and certainty
And sanctity are best.

title: Poem 924
text: VII.

title: Poem 925
text: THE BUTTERFLY'S DAY.

title: From cocoon forth a butterfly
text:
As lady from her door
Emerged – a summer afternoon –
Repairing everywhere,

title: Without design, that I could trace,
text:
Except to stray abroad
On miscellaneous enterprise
The clovers understood.

title: Her pretty parasol was seen
text:
Contracting in a field
Where men made hay, then struggling hard
With an opposing cloud,

title: Where parties, phantom as herself,
text:
To Nowhere seemed to go
In purposeless circumference,
As 't were a tropic show.

title: And notwithstanding bee that worked,
text:
And flower that zealous blew,
This audience of idleness
Disdained them, from the sky,

title: Till sundown crept, a steady tide,
text:
And men that made the hay,
And afternoon, and butterfly,
Extinguished in its sea.

title: Poem 932
text: VIII.

title: Poem 933
text: THE BLUEBIRD.

title: Before you thought of spring,
text:
Except as a surmise,
You see, God bless his suddenness,
A fellow in the skies
Of independent hues,
A little weather–worn,
Inspiriting habiliments
Of indigo and brown.

title: With specimens of song,
text:
As if for you to choose,
Discretion in the interval,
With gay delays he goes
To some superior tree
Without a single leaf,
And shouts for joy to nobody
But his seraphic self!

title: Poem 936
text: IX.

title: Poem 937
text: APRIL.

title: An altered look about the hills;
Poem 939
text: X.

title: Poem 940
text: THE SLEEPING FLOWERS.

title: "Whose are the little beds," I asked,
text:
"Which in the valleys lie?"
Some shook their heads, and others smiled,
And no one made reply.

title: "Perhaps they did not hear," I said;
text:
"I will inquire again.
Whose are the beds, the tiny beds
So thick upon the plain?"

title: "'T is daisy in the shortest;
text:
A little farther on,
Nearest the door to wake the first,
Little leontodon.

title: "'T is iris, sir, and aster,
text:
Anemone and bell,
Batschia in the blanket red,
And chubby daffodil."

title: Meanwhile at many cradles
text:
Her busy foot she plied,
Humming the quaintest lullaby
That ever rocked a child.

title: "Hush! Epigea wakens! –
text:
The crocus stirs her lids,
Rhodora's cheek is crimson, –
She's dreaming of the woods."

title: Then, turning from them, reverent,
Poem 948
text: XI.

title: Poem 949
text: MY ROSE.

title: Pigmy seraphs gone astray,
text:
Velvet people from Vevay,
Belles from some lost summer day,
Bees' exclusive coterie.
Paris could not lay the fold
Belted down with emerald;
Venice could not show a cheek
Of a tint so lustrous meek.
Never such an ambuscade
As of brier and leaf displayed
For my little damask maid.
I had rather wear her grace
Than an earl's distinguished face;
I had rather dwell like her
Than be Duke of Exeter
Royalty enough for me
To subdue the bumble–bee!

title: Poem 951
text: XII.

title: Poem 952
text: THE ORIOLE'S SECRET.

title: To hear an oriole sing
text:
May be a common thing,
Or only a divine.

title: It is not of the bird
text:
Who sings the same, unheard,
As unto crowd.

title: The fashion of the ear
text:
Attireth that it hear
In dun or fair.

title: So whether it be rune,
text:
Or whether it be none,
Is of within;

title: The "tune is in the tree,"
text:
The sceptic showeth me;
"No, sir! In thee!"

title: Poem 958
text: XIII.

title: Poem 959
text: THE ORIOLE.

title: One of the ones that Midas touched,
text:
Who failed to touch us all,
Was that confiding prodigal,
The blissful oriole.

title: So drunk, he disavows it
A pleader, a dissembler,
The Jesuit of orchards,
text:
He cheats as he enchants
Of an entire attar
For his decamping wants.

title: The splendor of a Burmah,
text:
The meteor of birds,
Departing like a pageant
Of ballads and of bards.

title: I never thought that Jason sought
But if there were a Jason,
text:
Tradition suffer me
Behold his lost emolument
Upon the apple–tree.

title: Poem 967
text: XIV.

title: Poem 968
text: IN SHADOW.

title: I dreaded that first robin so,
text:
But he is mastered now,
And I 'm accustomed to him grown, –
He hurts a little, though.

title: I thought if I could only live
text:
Till that first shout got by,
Not all pianos in the woods
Had power to mangle me.

title: I dared not meet the daffodils,
text:
For fear their yellow gown
Would pierce me with a fashion
So foreign to my own.

title: I wished the grass would hurry,
text:
So when 't was time to see,
He 'd be too tall, the tallest one
Could stretch to look at me.

title: I could not bear the bees should come,
text:
I wished they 'd stay away
In those dim countries where they go:
What word had they for me?

title: They 're here, though; not a creature failed,
text:
No blossom stayed away
In gentle deference to me,
The Queen of Calvary.

title: Each one salutes me as he goes,
text:
And I my childish plumes
Lift, in bereaved acknowledgment
Of their unthinking drums.

title: Poem 976
text: XV.

title: Poem 977
text: THE HUMMING–BIRD.

title: A route of evanescence
text:
With a revolving wheel;
A resonance of emerald,
A rush of cochineal;
And every blossom on the bush
Adjusts its tumbled head, –
The mail from Tunis, probably,
An easy morning's ride.

title: Poem 979
text: XVI.

title: Poem 980
text: SECRETS.

title: The skies can't keep their secret!
text:
They tell it to the hills –
The hills just tell the orchards –
And they the daffodils!

title: A bird, by chance, that goes that way
text:
Soft overheard the whole.
If I should bribe the little bird,
Who knows but she would tell?

title: I think I won't, however,
So keep your secret, Father!
text:
I would not, if I could,
Know what the sapphire fellows do,
In your new–fashioned world!

title: Poem 985
text: XVII.

title: Who robbed the woods,
text:
The trusting woods?
The unsuspecting trees
Brought out their burrs and mosses
His fantasy to please.
He scanned their trinkets, curious,
He grasped, he bore away.
What will the solemn hemlock,
What will the fir–tree say?

title: Poem 987
text: XVIII.

title: Poem 988
text: TWO VOYAGERS.

title: Two butterflies went out at noon
And then together bore away
text:
Upon a shining sea, –
Though never yet, in any port,
Their coming mentioned be.

title: If spoken by the distant bird,
text:
If met in ether sea
By frigate or by merchantman,
Report was not to me.

title: Poem 992
text: XIX.

title: Poem 993
text: BY THE SEA.

title: I started early, took my dog,
And frigates in the upper floor
text:
Extended hempen hands,
Presuming me to be a mouse
Aground, upon the sands.

title: But no man moved me till the tide
text:
Went past my simple shoe,
And past my apron and my belt,
And past my bodice too,

title: And made as he would eat me up
text:
As wholly as a dew
Upon a dandelion's sleeve –
And then I started too.

title: And he – he followed close behind;
text:
I felt his silver heel
Upon my ankle, – then my shoes
Would overflow with pearl.

title: Until we met the solid town,
Poem 1000
text: XX.

title: Poem 1001
text: OLD–FASHIONED.

title: Arcturus is his other name, –
text:
I'd rather call him star!
It's so unkind of science
To go and interfere!

title: I pull a flower from the woods, –
text:
A monster with a glass
Computes the stamens in a breath,
And has her in a class.

title: Whereas I took the butterfly
text:
Aforetime in my hat,
He sits erect in cabinets,
The clover–bells forgot.

title: What once was heaven, is zenith now.
text:
Where I proposed to go
When time's brief masquerade was done,
Is mapped, and charted too!

title: What if the poles should frisk about
text:
And stand upon their heads!
I hope I 'm ready for the worst,
Whatever prank betides!

title: Perhaps the kingdom of Heaven 's changed!
text:
I hope the children there
Won't be new–fashioned when I come,
And laugh at me, and stare!

title: I hope the father in the skies
text:
Will lift his little girl, –
Old–fashioned, naughty, everything, –
Over the stile of pearl!

title: Poem 1009
text: XXI.

title: Poem 1010
text: A TEMPEST.

title: An awful tempest mashed the air,
The creatures chuckled on the roofs
text:
And whistled in the air,
And shook their fists and gnashed their teeth.
And swung their frenzied hair.

title: The morning lit, the birds arose;
text:
The monster's faded eyes
Turned slowly to his native coast,
And peace was Paradise!

title: Poem 1014
text: XXII.

title: Poem 1015
text: THE SEA.

title: An everywhere of silver,
text:
With ropes of sand
To keep it from effacing
The track called land.

title: Poem 1017
text: XXIII.

title: Poem 1018
text: IN THE GARDEN.

title: A bird came down the walk:
And then he drank a dew
text:
From a convenient grass,
And then hopped sidewise to the wall
To let a beetle pass.

title: He glanced with rapid eyes
Like one in danger; cautious,
text:
I offered him a crumb,
And he unrolled his feathers
And rowed him softer home

title: Than oars divide the ocean,
text:
Too silver for a seam,
Or butterflies, off banks of noon,
Leap, splashless, as they swim.

title: Poem 1024
text: XXIV.

title: Poem 1025
text: THE SNAKE.

title: A narrow fellow in the grass
The grass divides as with a comb,
He likes a boggy acre,
text:
A floor too cool for corn.
Yet when a child, and barefoot,
I more than once, at morn,

title: Have passed, I thought, a whip–lash
text:
Unbraiding in the sun, –
When, stooping to secure it,
It wrinkled, and was gone.

title: Several of nature's people
But never met this fellow,
text:
Attended or alone,
Without a tighter breathing,
And zero at the bone.

title: Poem 1032
text: XXV.

title: Poem 1033
text: THE MUSHROOM.

title: The mushroom is the elf of plants,
As if it tarried always;
text:
And yet its whole career
Is shorter than a snake's delay,
And fleeter than a tare.

title: 'T is vegetation's juggler,
I feel as if the grass were pleased
Had nature any outcast face,
text:
Could she a son contemn,
Had nature an Iscariot,
That mushroom, – it is him.

title: Poem 1039
text: XXVI.

title: Poem 1040
text: THE STORM.

title: There came a wind like a bugle;
text:
It quivered through the grass,
And a green chill upon the heat
So ominous did pass
We barred the windows and the doors
As from an emerald ghost;
The doom's electric moccason
That very instant passed.
On a strange mob of panting trees,
And fences fled away,
And rivers where the houses ran
The living looked that day.
The bell within the steeple wild
The flying tidings whirled.
How much can come
And much can go,
And yet abide the world!

title: Poem 1042
text: XXVII.

title: Poem 1043
text: THE SPIDER.

title: A spider sewed at night
text:
Without a light
Upon an arc of white.
If ruff it was of dame
Or shroud of gnome,
Himself, himself inform.
Of immortality
His strategy
Was physiognomy.

title: Poem 1045
text: XXVIII.

title: I know a place where summer strives
text:
With such a practised frost,
She each year leads her daisies back,
Recording briefly, "Lost."

title: But when the south wind stirs the pools
text:
And struggles in the lanes,
Her heart misgives her for her vow,
And she pours soft refrains

title: Into the lap of adamant,
text:
And spices, and the dew,
That stiffens quietly to quartz,
Upon her amber shoe.

title: Poem 1049
text: XXIX.

title: The one that could repeat the summer day
text:
Were greater than itself, though he
Minutest of mankind might be.
And who could reproduce the sun,
At period of going down –
The lingering and the stain, I mean –
When Orient has been outgrown,
And Occident becomes unknown,
His name remain.

title: Poem 1051
text: XXX.

title: Poem 1052
text: THE WIND'S VISIT.

title: The wind tapped like a tired man,
text:
And like a host, "Come in,"
I boldly answered; entered then
My residence within

title: A rapid, footless guest,
text:
To offer whom a chair
Were as impossible as hand
A sofa to the air.

title: No bone had he to bind him,
text:
His speech was like the push
Of numerous humming–birds at once
From a superior bush.

title: His countenance a billow,
text:
His fingers, if he pass,
Let go a music, as of tunes
Blown tremulous in glass.

title: He visited, still flitting;
text:
Then, like a timid man,
Again he tapped – 't was flurriedly –
And I became alone.

title: Poem 1058
text: XXXI.

title: Nature rarer uses yellow
Spending scarlet like a woman,
text:
Yellow she affords
Only scantly and selectly,
Like a lover's words.

title: Poem 1061
text: XXXII.

title: Poem 1062
text: GOSSIP.

title: The leaves, like women, interchange
The parties in both cases
text:
Enjoining secrecy, –
Inviolable compact
To notoriety.

title: Poem 1065
text: XXXIII.

title: Poem 1066
text: SIMPLICITY.

title: How happy is the little stone
text:
That rambles in the road alone,
And doesn't care about careers,
And exigencies never fears;
Whose coat of elemental brown
A passing universe put on;
And independent as the sun,
Associates or glows alone,
Fulfilling absolute decree
In casual simplicity.

title: Poem 1068
text: XXXIV.

title: Poem 1069
text: STORM.

title: It sounded as if the streets were running,
text:
And then the streets stood still.
Eclipse was all we could see at the window,
And awe was all we could feel.

title: By and by the boldest stole out of his covert,
text:
To see if time was there.
Nature was in her beryl apron,
Mixing fresher air.

title: Poem 1072
text: XXXV.

title: Poem 1073
text: THE RAT.

title: The rat is the concisest tenant.
text:
He pays no rent, –
Repudiates the obligation,
On schemes intent.

title: Balking our wit
text:
To sound or circumvent,
Hate cannot harm
A foe so reticent.

title: Neither decree
text:
Prohibits him,
Lawful as
Equilibrium.

title: Poem 1077
text: XXXVI.

title: Frequently the woods are pink,
Oft a head is crested
text:
I was wont to see,
And as oft a cranny
Where it used to be.

title: And the earth, they tell me,
text:
On its axis turned, –
Wonderful rotation
By but twelve performed!

title: Poem 1081
text: XXXVII.

title: Poem 1082
text: A THUNDER–STORM.

title: The wind begun to rock the grass
text:
With threatening tunes and low, –
He flung a menace at the earth,
A menace at the sky.

title: The leaves unhooked themselves from trees
The wagons quickened on the streets,
The birds put up the bars to nests,
That held the dams had parted hold,
text:
The waters wrecked the sky,
But overlooked my father's house,
Just quartering a tree.

title: Poem 1088
text: XXXVIII.

title: Poem 1089
text: WITH FLOWERS.

title: South winds jostle them,
text:
Bumblebees come,
Hover, hesitate,
Drink, and are gone.

title: Butterflies pause
Poem 1092
text: XXXIX.

title: Poem 1093
text: SUNSET.

title: Where ships of purple gently toss
text:
On seas of daffodil,
Fantastic sailors mingle,
And then – the wharf is still.

title: Poem 1095
text: XL.

title: She sweeps with many–colored brooms,
You dropped a purple ravelling in,
And still she plies her spotted brooms,
text:
And still the aprons fly,
Till brooms fade softly into stars –
And then I come away.

title: Poem 1099
text: XLI.

title: Like mighty footlights burned the red
text:
At bases of the trees, –
The far theatricals of day
Exhibiting to these.

title: 'T was universe that did applaud
text:
While, chiefest of the crowd,
Enabled by his royal dress,
Myself distinguished God.

title: Poem 1102
text: XLII.

title: Poem 1103
text: PROBLEMS.

title: Bring me the sunset in a cup,
text:
Reckon the morning's flagons up,
And say how many dew;
Tell me how far the morning leaps,
Tell me what time the weaver sleeps
Who spun the breadths of blue!

title: Write me how many notes there be
text:
In the new robin's ecstasy
Among astonished boughs;
How many trips the tortoise makes,
How many cups the bee partakes, –
The debauchee of dews!

title: Also, who laid the rainbow's piers,
text:
Also, who leads the docile spheres
By withes of supple blue?
Whose fingers string the stalactite,
Who counts the wampum of the night,
To see that none is due?

title: Who built this little Alban house
text:
And shut the windows down so close
My spirit cannot see?
Who 'll let me out some gala day,
With implements to fly away,
Passing pomposity?

title: Poem 1108
text: XLIII.

title: Poem 1109
text: THE JUGGLER OF DAY.

title: Blazing in gold and quenching in purple,
Stooping as low as the otter's window,
text:
Touching the roof and tinting the barn,
Kissing her bonnet to the meadow, –
And the juggler of day is gone!

title: Poem 1112
text: XLIV.

title: Poem 1113
text: MY CRICKET.

title: Farther in summer than the birds,
text:
Pathetic from the grass,
A minor nation celebrates
Its unobtrusive mass.

title: No ordinance is seen,
text:
So gradual the grace,
A pensive custom it becomes,
Enlarging loneliness.

title: Antiquest felt at noon
text:
When August, burning low,
Calls forth this spectral canticle,
Repose to typify.

title: Remit as yet no grace,
text:
No furrow on the glow,
Yet a druidic difference
Enhances nature now.

title: Poem 1118
text: XLV.

title: As imperceptibly as grief
text:
The summer lapsed away, –
Too imperceptible, at last,
To seem like perfidy.

title: A quietness distilled,
text:
As twilight long begun,
Or Nature, spending with herself
Sequestered afternoon.

title: The dusk drew earlier in,
text:
The morning foreign shone, –
A courteous, yet harrowing grace,
As guest who would be gone.

title: And thus, without a wing,
text:
Or service of a keel,
Our summer made her light escape
Into the beautiful.

title: Poem 1123
text: XLVI.

title: It can't be summer, – that got through;
It can't be dying, – it's too rouge, –
text:
The dead shall go in white.
So sunset shuts my question down
With clasps of chrysolite.

title: Poem 1126
text: XLVII.

title: Poem 1127
text: SUMMER'S OBSEQUIES.

title: The gentian weaves her fringes,
text:
The maple's loom is red.
My departing blossoms
Obviate parade.

title: A brief, but patient illness,
It was a short procession, –
text:
The bobolink was there,
An aged bee addressed us,
And then we knelt in prayer.

title: We trust that she was willing, –
text:
We ask that we may be.
Summer, sister, seraph,
Let us go with thee!

title: In the name of the bee
text:
And of the butterfly
And of the breeze, amen!

title: Poem 1133
text: XLVIII.

title: Poem 1134
text: FRINGED GENTIAN.

title: God made a little gentian;
text:
It tried to be a rose
And failed, and all the summer laughed.
But just before the snows
There came a purple creature
That ravished all the hill;
And summer hid her forehead,
And mockery was still.
The frosts were her condition;
The Tyrian would not come
Until the North evoked it.
"Creator! shall I bloom?"

title: Poem 1136
text: XLIX.

title: Poem 1137
text: NOVEMBER.

title: Besides the autumn poets sing,
text:
A few prosaic days
A little this side of the snow
And that side of the haze.

title: A few incisive mornings,
text:
A few ascetic eyes, –
Gone Mr. Bryant's golden–rod,
And Mr. Thomson's sheaves.

title: Still is the bustle in the brook,
Perhaps a squirrel may remain,
text:
My sentiments to share.
Grant me, O Lord, a sunny mind,
Thy windy will to bear!

title: Poem 1142
text: L.

title: Poem 1143
text: THE SNOW.

title: It sifts from leaden sieves,
text:
It powders all the wood,
It fills with alabaster wool
The wrinkles of the road.

title: It makes an even face
text:
Of mountain and of plain, –
Unbroken forehead from the east
Unto the east again.

title: It reaches to the fence,
On stump and stack and stem, –
text:
The summer's empty room,
Acres of seams where harvests were,
Recordless, but for them.

title: It ruffles wrists of posts,
text:
As ankles of a queen, –
Then stills its artisans like ghosts,
Denying they have been.

title: Poem 1149
text: LI.

title: Poem 1150
text: THE BLUE JAY.

title: No brigadier throughout the year
text:
So civic as the jay.
A neighbor and a warrior too,
With shrill felicity

title: Pursuing winds that censure us
text:
A February day,
The brother of the universe
Was never blown away.

title: The snow and he are intimate;
text:
I 've often seen them play
When heaven looked upon us all
With such severity,

title: I felt apology were due
text:
To an insulted sky,
Whose pompous frown was nutriment
To their temerity.

title: The pillow of this daring head
His character a tonic,
Poem 1157
text: IV. TIME AND ETERNITY.

title: Poem 1158
text: I.

title: Let down the bars, O Death!
text:
The tired flocks come in
Whose bleating ceases to repeat,
Whose wandering is done.

title: Thine is the stillest night,
Poem 1161
text: II.

title: Going to heaven!
text:
I don't know when,
Pray do not ask me how, –
Indeed, I 'm too astonished
To think of answering you!
Going to heaven! –
How dim it sounds!
And yet it will be done
As sure as flocks go home at night
Unto the shepherd's arm!

title: Perhaps you 're going too!
text:
Who knows?
If you should get there first,
Save just a little place for me
Close to the two I lost!

title: The smallest "robe" will fit me,
text:
And just a bit of "crown;"
For you know we do not mind our dress
When we are going home.

title: I 'm glad I don't believe it,
text:
For it would stop my breath,
And I 'd like to look a little more
At such a curious earth!
I am glad they did believe it
Whom I have never found
Since the mighty autumn afternoon
I left them in the ground.

title: Poem 1166
text: III.

title: At least to pray is left, is left.
text:
O Jesus! in the air
I know not which thy chamber is, –
I 'm knocking everywhere.

title: Thou stirrest earthquake in the South,
Poem 1169
text: IV.

title: Poem 1170
text: EPITAPH.

title: Step lightly on this narrow spot!
text:
The broadest land that grows
Is not so ample as the breast
These emerald seams enclose.

title: Step lofty; for this name is told
text:
As far as cannon dwell,
Or flag subsist, or fame export
Her deathless syllable.

title: Poem 1173
text: V.

title: Morns like these we parted;
text:
Noons like these she rose,
Fluttering first, then firmer,
To her fair repose.

title: Never did she lisp it,
Till the evening, nearing,
text:
One the shutters drew –
Quick! a sharper rustling!
And this linnet flew!

title: Poem 1177
text: VI.

title: A death–blow is a life–blow to some
Poem 1179
text: VII.

title: I read my sentence steadily,
text:
Reviewed it with my eyes,
To see that I made no mistake
In its extremest clause, –

title: The date, and manner of the shame;
text:
And then the pious form
That "God have mercy" on the soul
The jury voted him.

title: I made my soul familiar
text:
With her extremity,
That at the last it should not be
A novel agony,

title: But she and Death, acquainted,
text:
Meet tranquilly as friends,
Salute and pass without a hint –
And there the matter ends.

title: Poem 1184
text: VIII.

title: I have not told my garden yet,
I will not name it in the street,
text:
For shops would stare, that I,
So shy, so very ignorant,
Should have the face to die.

title: The hillsides must not know it,
text:
Where I have rambled so,
Nor tell the loving forests
The day that I shall go,

title: Nor lisp it at the table,
text:
Nor heedless by the way
Hint that within the riddle
One will walk to–day!

title: Poem 1189
text: IX.

title: Poem 1190
text: THE BATTLE–FIELD.

title: They dropped like flakes, they dropped like stars,
text:
Like petals from a rose,
When suddenly across the June
A wind with fingers goes.

title: They perished in the seamless grass, –
Poem 1193
text: X.

title: The only ghost I ever saw
text:
Was dressed in mechlin, – so;
He wore no sandal on his foot,
And stepped like flakes of snow.
His gait was soundless, like the bird,
But rapid, like the roe;
His fashions quaint, mosaic,
Or, haply, mistletoe.

title: His conversation seldom,
text:
His laughter like the breeze
That dies away in dimples
Among the pensive trees.
Our interview was transient,–
Of me, himself was shy;
And God forbid I look behind
Since that appalling day!

title: Poem 1196
text: XI.

title: Some, too fragile for winter winds,
text:
The thoughtful grave encloses, –
Tenderly tucking them in from frost
Before their feet are cold.

title: Never the treasures in her nest
text:
The cautious grave exposes,
Building where schoolboy dare not look
And sportsman is not bold.

title: This covert have all the children
Poem 1200
text: XII.

title: As by the dead we love to sit,
text:
Become so wondrous dear,
As for the lost we grapple,
Though all the rest are here, –

title: In broken mathematics
text:
We estimate our prize,
Vast, in its fading ratio,
To our penurious eyes!

title: Poem 1203
text: XIII.

title: Poem 1204
text: MEMORIALS.

title: Death sets a thing significant
text:
The eye had hurried by,
Except a perished creature
Entreat us tenderly

title: To ponder little workmanships
text:
In crayon or in wool,
With "This was last her fingers did,"
Industrious until

title: The thimble weighed too heavy,
text:
The stitches stopped themselves,
And then 't was put among the dust
Upon the closet shelves.

title: A book I have, a friend gave,
text:
Whose pencil, here and there,
Had notched the place that pleased him, –
At rest his fingers are.

title: Now, when I read, I read not,
text:
For interrupting tears
Obliterate the etchings
Too costly for repairs.

title: Poem 1210
text: XIV.

title: I went to heaven, –
text:
'T was a small town,
Lit with a ruby,
Lathed with down.
Stiller than the fields
At the full dew,
Beautiful as pictures
No man drew.
People like the moth,
Of mechlin, frames,
Duties of gossamer,
And eider names.
Almost contented
I could be
'Mong such unique
Society.

title: Poem 1212
text: XV.

title: Their height in heaven comforts not,
The house of supposition,
text:
The glimmering frontier
That skirts the acres of perhaps,
To me shows insecure.

title: The wealth I had contented me;
text:
If 't was a meaner size,
Then I had counted it until
It pleased my narrow eyes

title: Better than larger values,
Poem 1217
text: XVI.

title: There is a shame of nobleness
text:
Confronting sudden pelf, –
A finer shame of ecstasy
Convicted of itself.

title: A best disgrace a brave man feels,
Poem 1220
text: XVII.

title: Poem 1221
text: TRIUMPH.

title: Triumph may be of several kinds.
text:
There 's triumph in the room
When that old imperator, Death,
By faith is overcome.

title: There 's triumph of the finer mind
text:
When truth, affronted long,
Advances calm to her supreme,
Her God her only throng.

title: A triumph when temptation's bribe
text:
Is slowly handed back,
One eye upon the heaven renounced
And one upon the rack.

title: Severer triumph, by himself
text:
Experienced, who can pass
Acquitted from that naked bar,
Jehovah's countenance!

title: Poem 1226
text: XVIII.

title: Pompless no life can pass away;
text:
The lowliest career
To the same pageant wends its way
As that exalted here.
How cordial is the mystery!
The hospitable pall
A "this way" beckons spaciously, –
A miracle for all!

title: Poem 1228
text: XIX.

title: I noticed people disappeared,
text:
When but a little child, –
Supposed they visited remote,
Or settled regions wild.

title: Now know I they both visited
text:
And settled regions wild,
But did because they died, – a fact
Withheld the little child!

title: Poem 1231
text: XX.

title: Poem 1232
text: FOLLOWING.

title: I had no cause to be awake,
text:
My best was gone to sleep,
And morn a new politeness took,
And failed to wake them up,

title: But called the others clear,
text:
And passed their curtains by.
Sweet morning, when I over–sleep,
Knock, recollect, for me!

title: I looked at sunrise once,
text:
And then I looked at them,
And wishfulness in me arose
For circumstance the same.

title: 'T was such an ample peace,
text:
It could not hold a sigh, –
'T was Sabbath with the bells divorced,
'T was sunset all the day.

title: So choosing but a gown
text:
And taking but a prayer,
The only raiment I should need,
I struggled, and was there.

title: Poem 1238
text: XXI.

title: If anybody's friend be dead,
text:
It 's sharpest of the theme
The thinking how they walked alive,
At such and such a time.

title: Their costume, of a Sunday,
text:
Some manner of the hair, –
A prank nobody knew but them,
Lost, in the sepulchre.

title: How warm they were on such a day:
text:
You almost feel the date,
So short way off it seems; and now,
They 're centuries from that.

title: How pleased they were at what you said;
text:
You try to touch the smile,
And dip your fingers in the frost:
When was it, can you tell,

title: You asked the company to tea,
text:
Acquaintance, just a few,
And chatted close with this grand thing
That don't remember you?

title: Past bows and invitations,
text:
Past interview, and vow,
Past what ourselves can estimate, –
That makes the quick of woe!

title: Poem 1245
text: XXII.

title: Poem 1246
text: THE JOURNEY.

title: Our journey had advanced;
text:
Our feet were almost come
To that odd fork in Being's road,
Eternity by term.

title: Our pace took sudden awe,
text:
Our feet reluctant led.
Before were cities, but between,
The forest of the dead.

title: Retreat was out of hope, –
text:
Behind, a sealed route,
Eternity's white flag before,
And God at every gate.

title: Poem 1250
text: XXIII.

title: Poem 1251
text: A COUNTRY BURIAL.

title: Ample make this bed.
Be its mattress straight,
Poem 1254
text: XXIV.

title: Poem 1255
text: GOING.

title: On such a night, or such a night,
text:
Would anybody care
If such a little figure
Slipped quiet from its chair,

title: So quiet, oh, how quiet!
text:
That nobody might know
But that the little figure
Rocked softer, to and fro?

title: On such a dawn, or such a dawn,
text:
Would anybody sigh
That such a little figure
Too sound asleep did lie

title: For chanticleer to wake it, –
text:
Or stirring house below,
Or giddy bird in orchard,
Or early task to do?

title: There was a little figure plump
text:
For every little knoll,
Busy needles, and spools of thread,
And trudging feet from school.

title: Playmates, and holidays, and nuts,
text:
And visions vast and small.
Strange that the feet so precious charged
Should reach so small a goal!

title: Poem 1262
text: XXV.

title: Essential oils are wrung:
text:
The attar from the rose
Is not expressed by suns alone,
It is the gift of screws.

title: The general rose decays;
text:
But this, in lady's drawer,
Makes summer when the lady lies
In ceaseless rosemary.

title: Poem 1265
text: XXVI.

title: I lived on dread; to those who know
text:
The stimulus there is
In danger, other impetus
Is numb and vital–less.

title: As 't were a spur upon the soul,
text:
A fear will urge it where
To go without the spectre's aid
Were challenging despair.

title: Poem 1268
text: XXVII.

title: If I should die,
text:
And you should live,
And time should gurgle on,
And morn should beam,
And noon should burn,
As it has usual done;
If birds should build as early,
And bees as bustling go, –
One might depart at option
From enterprise below!
'T is sweet to know that stocks will stand
When we with daisies lie,
That commerce will continue,
And trades as briskly fly.
It makes the parting tranquil
And keeps the soul serene,
That gentlemen so sprightly
Conduct the pleasing scene!

title: Poem 1270
text: XXVIII.

title: Poem 1271
text: AT LENGTH.

title: Her final summer was it,
A further force of life
text:
Developed from within, –
When Death lit all the shortness up,
And made the hurry plain.

title: We wondered at our blindness, –
text:
When nothing was to see
But her Carrara guide–post, –
At our stupidity,

title: When, duller than our dullness,
text:
The busy darling lay,
So busy was she, finishing,
So leisurely were we!

title: Poem 1276
text: XXIX.

title: Poem 1277
text: GHOSTS.

title: One need not be a chamber to be haunted,
Far safer, of a midnight meeting
text:
External ghost,
Than an interior confronting
That whiter host.

title: Far safer through an Abbey gallop,
text:
The stones achase,
Than, moonless, one's own self encounter
In lonesome place.

title: Ourself, behind ourself concealed,
The prudent carries a revolver,
text:
He bolts the door,
O'erlooking a superior spectre
More near.

title: Poem 1283
text: XXX.

title: Poem 1284
text: VANISHED.

title: She died, – this was the way she died;
text:
And when her breath was done,
Took up her simple wardrobe
And started for the sun.

title: Her little figure at the gate
text:
The angels must have spied,
Since I could never find her
Upon the mortal side.

title: Poem 1287
text: XXXI.

title: Poem 1288
text: PRECEDENCE.

title: Wait till the majesty of Death
text:
Invests so mean a brow!
Almost a powdered footman
Might dare to touch it now!

title: Wait till in everlasting robes
text:
This democrat is dressed,
Then prate about "preferment"
And "station" and the rest!

title: Around this quiet courtier
text:
Obsequious angels wait!
Full royal is his retinue,
Full purple is his state!

title: A lord might dare to lift the hat
text:
To such a modest clay,
Since that my Lord, "the Lord of lords"
Receives unblushingly!

title: Poem 1293
text: XXXII.

title: Poem 1294
text: GONE.

title: Went up a year this evening!
text:
I recollect it well!
Amid no bells nor bravos
The bystanders will tell!
Cheerful, as to the village,
Tranquil, as to repose,
Chastened, as to the chapel,
This humble tourist rose.
Did not talk of returning,
Alluded to no time
When, were the gales propitious,
We might look for him;
Was grateful for the roses
In life's diverse bouquet,
Talked softly of new species
To pick another day.

title: Beguiling thus the wonder,
text:
The wondrous nearer drew;
Hands bustled at the moorings –
The crowd respectful grew.
Ascended from our vision
To countenances new!
A difference, a daisy,
Is all the rest I knew!

title: Poem 1297
text: XXXIII.

title: Poem 1298
text: REQUIEM.

title: Taken from men this morning,
text:
Carried by men to–day,
Met by the gods with banners
Who marshalled her away.

title: One little maid from playmates,
Far as the east from even,
text:
Dim as the border star, –
Courtiers quaint, in kingdoms,
Our departed are.

title: Poem 1302
text: XXXIV.

title: What inn is this
text:
Where for the night
Peculiar traveller comes?
Who is the landlord?
Where the maids?
Behold, what curious rooms!
No ruddy fires on the hearth,
No brimming tankards flow.
Necromancer, landlord,
Who are these below?

title: Poem 1304
text: XXXV.

title: It was not death, for I stood up,
It was not frost, for on my flesh
text:
I felt siroccos crawl, –
Nor fire, for just my marble feet
Could keep a chancel cool.

title: And yet it tasted like them all;
text:
The figures I have seen
Set orderly, for burial,
Reminded me of mine,

title: As if my life were shaven
When everything that ticked has stopped,
text:
And space stares, all around,
Or grisly frosts, first autumn morns,
Repeal the beating ground.

title: But most like chaos, – stopless, cool, –
text:
Without a chance or spar,
Or even a report of land
To justify despair.

title: Poem 1311
text: XXXVI.

title: Poem 1312
text: TILL THE END.

title: I should not dare to leave my friend,
If I should disappoint the eyes
If I should stab the patient faith
text:
So sure I 'd come – so sure I 'd come,
It listening, listening, went to sleep
Telling my tardy name, –

title: My heart would wish it broke before,
text:
Since breaking then, since breaking then,
Were useless as next morning's sun,
Where midnight frosts had lain!

title: Poem 1317
text: XXXVII.

title: Poem 1318
text: VOID.

title: Great streets of silence led away
By clocks 't was morning, and for night
Poem 1321
text: XXXVIII.

title: A throe upon the features
text:
A hurry in the breath,
An ecstasy of parting
Denominated "Death," –

title: An anguish at the mention,
text:
Which, when to patience grown,
I 've known permission given
To rejoin its own.

title: Poem 1324
text: XXXIX.

title: Poem 1325
text: SAVED!

title: Of tribulation these are they
All these did conquer; but the ones
text:
Who overcame most times
Wear nothing commoner than snow,
No ornament but palms.

title: Surrender is a sort unknown
Our panting ankle barely gained
Poem 1330
text: XL.

title: I think just how my shape will rise
text:
When I shall be forgiven,
Till hair and eyes and timid head
Are out of sight, in heaven.

title: I think just how my lips will weigh
text:
With shapeless, quivering prayer
That you, so late, consider me,
The sparrow of your care.

title: I mind me that of anguish sent,
text:
Some drifts were moved away
Before my simple bosom broke, –
And why not this, if they?

title: And so, until delirious borne
text:
I con that thing, – "forgiven," –
Till with long fright and longer trust
I drop my heart, unshriven!

title: Poem 1335
text: XLI.

title: Poem 1336
text: THE FORGOTTEN GRAVE.

title: After a hundred years
text:
Nobody knows the place, –
Agony, that enacted there,
Motionless as peace.

title: Weeds triumphant ranged,
text:
Strangers strolled and spelled
At the lone orthography
Of the elder dead.

title: Winds of summer fields
text:
Recollect the way, –
Instinct picking up the key
Dropped by memory.

title: Poem 1340
text: XLII.

title: Lay this laurel on the one
text:
Too intrinsic for renown.
Laurel! veil your deathless tree, –
Him you chasten, that is he!

title: POEMS
text:
by EMILY DICKINSON
Third Series

title: Poem 1343
text: Edited by

title: Poem 1344
text: MABEL LOOMIS TODD

title: It's all I have to bring to–day,
text:
This, and my heart beside,
This, and my heart, and all the fields,
And all the meadows wide.
Be sure you count, should I forget, –
Some one the sum could tell, –
This, and my heart, and all the bees
Which in the clover dwell.

title: Poem 1346
text: PREFACE.

title: Poem 1347
text: The intellectual activity of Emily Dickinson was so great that a large and characteristic choice is still possible among her literary material, and this third volume of her verses is put forth in response to the repeated wish of the admirers of her peculiar genius. Much of Emily Dickinson's prose was rhythmic, –even rhymed, though frequently not set apart in lines.

title: Poem 1348
text: Also many verses, written as such, were sent to friends in letters; these were published in 1894, in the volumes of her Letters. It has not been necessary, however, to include them in this Series, and all have been omitted, except three or four exceptionally strong ones, as "A Book," and "With Flowers."

title: Poem 1349
text: There is internal evidence that many of the poems were simply spontaneous flashes of insight, apparently unrelated to outward circumstance. Others, however, had an obvious personal origin; for example, the verses "I had a Guinea golden," which seem to have been sent to some friend travelling in Europe, as a dainty reminder of letter–writing delinquencies. The surroundings in which any of Emily Dickinson's verses are known to have been written usually serve to explain them clearly; but in general the present volume is full of thoughts needing no interpretation to those who apprehend this scintillating spirit.

title: Poem 1350
text: M. L. T.

title: Poem 1351
text: AMHERST, October, 1896.

title: Poem 1352
text: I. LIFE.

title: Poem 1353
text: I.

title: Poem 1354
text: REAL RICHES.

title: 'T is little I could care for pearls
Or gold, who am the Prince of Mines;
text:
Or diamonds, when I see
A diadem to fit a dome
Continual crowning me.

title: Poem 1357
text: II.

title: Poem 1358
text: SUPERIORITY TO FATE.

title: Superiority to fate
text:
Is difficult to learn.
'T is not conferred by any,
But possible to earn

title: A pittance at a time,
text:
Until, to her surprise,
The soul with strict economy
Subsists till Paradise.

title: Poem 1361
text: III.

title: Poem 1362
text: HOPE.

title: Hope is a subtle glutton;
His is the halcyon table
text:
That never seats but one,
And whatsoever is consumed
The same amounts remain.

title: Poem 1365
text: IV.

title: Poem 1366
text: FORBIDDEN FRUIT.

title: Poem 1367
text: I.

title: Forbidden fruit a flavor has
Poem 1369
text: V.

title: Poem 1370
text: FORBIDDEN FRUIT.

title: Poem 1371
text: II.

title: Heaven is what I cannot reach!
text:
The apple on the tree,
Provided it do hopeless hang,
That 'heaven' is, to me.

title: The color on the cruising cloud,
text:
The interdicted ground
Behind the hill, the house behind, –
There Paradise is found!

title: Poem 1374
text: VI.

title: Poem 1375
text: A WORD.

title: A word is dead
text:
When it is said,
Some say.
I say it just
Begins to live
That day.

title: Poem 1377
text: VII.

title: To venerate the simple days
text:
Which lead the seasons by,
Needs but to remember
That from you or me
They may take the trifle
Termed mortality!

title: To invest existence with a stately air,
text:
Needs but to remember
That the acorn there
Is the egg of forests
For the upper air!

title: Poem 1380
text: VIII.

title: Poem 1381
text: LIFE'S TRADES.

title: It's such a little thing to weep,
Poem 1383
text: IX.

title: Drowning is not so pitiful
text:
As the attempt to rise.
Three times, 't is said, a sinking man
Comes up to face the skies,
And then declines forever
To that abhorred abode
Where hope and he part company, –
For he is grasped of God.
The Maker's cordial visage,
However good to see,
Is shunned, we must admit it,
Like an adversity.

title: Poem 1385
text: X.

title: How still the bells in steeples stand,
text:
Till, swollen with the sky,
They leap upon their silver feet
In frantic melody!

title: Poem 1387
text: XI.

title: If the foolish call them 'flowers,'
text:
Need the wiser tell?
If the savans 'classify' them,
It is just as well!

title: Those who read the Revelations
text:
Must not criticise
Those who read the same edition
With beclouded eyes!

title: Could we stand with that old Moses
text:
Canaan denied, –
Scan, like him, the stately landscape
On the other side, –

title: Doubtless we should deem superfluous
text:
Many sciences
Not pursued by learnèd angels
In scholastic skies!

title: Low amid that glad Belles lettres
text:
Grant that we may stand,
Stars, amid profound Galaxies,
At that grand 'Right hand'!

title: Poem 1393
text: XII.

title: Poem 1394
text: A SYLLABLE.

title: Could mortal lip divine
text:
The undeveloped freight
Of a delivered syllable,
'T would crumble with the weight.

title: Poem 1396
text: XIII.

title: Poem 1397
text: PARTING.

title: My life closed twice before its close;
text:
It yet remains to see
If Immortality unveil
A third event to me,

title: So huge, so hopeless to conceive,
text:
As these that twice befell.
Parting is all we know of heaven,
And all we need of hell.

title: Poem 1400
text: XIV.

title: Poem 1401
text: ASPIRATION.

title: We never know how high we are
The heroism we recite
text:
Would be a daily thing,
Did not ourselves the cubits warp
For fear to be a king.

title: Poem 1404
text: XV.

title: Poem 1405
text: THE INEVITABLE.

title: While I was fearing it, it came,
text:
But came with less of fear,
Because that fearing it so long
Had almost made it dear.
There is a fitting a dismay,
A fitting a despair.
'Tis harder knowing it is due,
Than knowing it is here.
The trying on the utmost,
The morning it is new,
Is terribler than wearing it
A whole existence through.

title: Poem 1407
text: XVI.

title: Poem 1408
text: A BOOK.

title: There is no frigate like a book
text:
To take us lands away,
Nor any coursers like a page
Of prancing poetry.
This traverse may the poorest take
Without oppress of toll;
How frugal is the chariot
That bears a human soul!

title: Poem 1410
text: XVII.

title: Who has not found the heaven below
text:
Will fail of it above.
God's residence is next to mine,
His furniture is love.

title: Poem 1412
text: XVIII.

title: Poem 1413
text: A PORTRAIT.

title: A face devoid of love or grace,
text:
A hateful, hard, successful face,
A face with which a stone
Would feel as thoroughly at ease
As were they old acquaintances, –
First time together thrown.

title: Poem 1415
text: XIX.

title: Poem 1416
text: I HAD A GUINEA GOLDEN.

title: I had a guinea golden;
text:
I lost it in the sand,
And though the sum was simple,
And pounds were in the land,
Still had it such a value
Unto my frugal eye,
That when I could not find it
I sat me down to sigh.

title: I had a crimson robin
text:
Who sang full many a day,
But when the woods were painted
He, too, did fly away.
Time brought me other robins, –
Their ballads were the same, –
Still for my missing troubadour
I kept the 'house at hame.'

title: I had a star in heaven;
text:
One Pleiad was its name,
And when I was not heeding
It wandered from the same.
And though the skies are crowded,
And all the night ashine,
I do not care about it,
Since none of them are mine.

title: My story has a moral:
text:
I have a missing friend, –
Pleiad its name, and robin,
And guinea in the sand, –
And when this mournful ditty,
Accompanied with tear,
Shall meet the eye of traitor
In country far from here,
Grant that repentance solemn
May seize upon his mind,
And he no consolation
Beneath the sun may find.

title: NOTE. – This poem may have had, like many others, a
text:
personal origin. It is more than probable that it was
sent to some friend travelling in Europe, a dainty
reminder of letter–writing delinquencies.

title: Poem 1422
text: XX.

title: Poem 1423
text: SATURDAY AFTERNOON.

title: From all the jails the boys and girls
text:
Ecstatically leap, –
Beloved, only afternoon
That prison doesn't keep.

title: They storm the earth and stun the air,
text:
A mob of solid bliss.
Alas! that frowns could lie in wait
For such a foe as this!

title: Poem 1426
text: XXI.

title: Few get enough, – enough is one;
text:
To that ethereal throng
Have not each one of us the right
To stealthily belong?

title: Poem 1428
text: XXII.

title: Upon the gallows hung a wretch,
text:
Too sullied for the hell
To which the law entitled him.
As nature's curtain fell
The one who bore him tottered in,
For this was woman's son.
''T was all I had,' she stricken gasped;
Oh, what a livid boon!

title: Poem 1430
text: XXIII.

title: Poem 1431
text: THE LOST THOUGHT.

title: I felt a clearing in my mind
The thought behind I strove to join
text:
Unto the thought before,
But sequence ravelled out of reach
Like balls upon a floor.

title: Poem 1434
text: XXIV.

title: Poem 1435
text: RETICENCE.

title: The reticent volcano keeps
If nature will not tell the tale
text:
Jehovah told to her,
Can human nature not survive
Without a listener?

title: Admonished by her buckled lips
text:
Let every babbler be.
The only secret people keep
Is Immortality.

title: Poem 1439
text: XXV.

title: Poem 1440
text: WITH FLOWERS.

title: If recollecting were forgetting,
text:
Then I remember not;
And if forgetting, recollecting,
How near I had forgot!
And if to miss were merry,
And if to mourn were gay,
How very blithe the fingers
That gathered these to–day!

title: Poem 1442
text: XXVI.

title: The farthest thunder that I heard
text:
Was nearer than the sky,
And rumbles still, though torrid noons
Have lain their missiles by.
The lightning that preceded it
Struck no one but myself,
But I would not exchange the bolt
For all the rest of life.
Indebtedness to oxygen
The chemist may repay,
But not the obligation
To electricity.
It founds the homes and decks the days,
And every clamor bright
Is but the gleam concomitant
Of that waylaying light.
The thought is quiet as a flake, –
A crash without a sound;
How life's reverberation
Its explanation found!

title: Poem 1444
text: XXVII.

title: On the bleakness of my lot
text:
Bloom I strove to raise.
Late, my acre of a rock
Yielded grape and maize.

title: Soil of flint if steadfast tilled
Poem 1447
text: XXVIII.

title: Poem 1448
text: CONTRAST.

title: A door just opened on a street –
text:
I, lost, was passing by –
An instant's width of warmth disclosed,
And wealth, and company.

title: The door as sudden shut, and I,
text:
I, lost, was passing by, –
Lost doubly, but by contrast most,
Enlightening misery.

title: Poem 1451
text: XXIX.

title: Poem 1452
text: FRIENDS.

title: Are friends delight or pain?
text:
Could bounty but remain
Riches were good.

title: But if they only stay
text:
Bolder to fly away,
Riches are sad.

title: Poem 1455
text: XXX.

title: Poem 1456
text: FIRE.

title: Ashes denote that fire was;
text:
Respect the grayest pile
For the departed creature's sake
That hovered there awhile.

title: Fire exists the first in light,
text:
And then consolidates, –
Only the chemist can disclose
Into what carbonates.

title: Poem 1459
text: XXXI.

title: Poem 1460
text: A MAN.

title: Fate slew him, but he did not drop;
text:
She felled – he did not fall –
Impaled him on her fiercest stakes –
He neutralized them all.

title: She stung him, sapped his firm advance,
text:
But, when her worst was done,
And he, unmoved, regarded her,
Acknowledged him a man.

title: Poem 1463
text: XXXII.

title: Poem 1464
text: VENTURES.

title: Finite to fail, but infinite to venture.
text:
For the one ship that struts the shore
Many's the gallant, overwhelmed creature
Nodding in navies nevermore.

title: Poem 1466
text: XXXIII.

title: Poem 1467
text: GRIEFS.

title: I measure every grief I meet
I wonder if they bore it long,
text:
Or did it just begin?
I could not tell the date of mine,
It feels so old a pain.

title: I wonder if it hurts to live,
text:
And if they have to try,
And whether, could they choose between,
They would not rather die.

title: I wonder if when years have piled –
Or would they go on aching still
text:
Through centuries above,
Enlightened to a larger pain
By contrast with the love.

title: The grieved are many, I am told;
text:
The reason deeper lies, –
Death is but one and comes but once,
And only nails the eyes.

title: There's grief of want, and grief of cold, –
text:
A sort they call 'despair;'
There's banishment from native eyes,
In sight of native air.

title: And though I may not guess the kind
text:
Correctly, yet to me
A piercing comfort it affords
In passing Calvary,

title: To note the fashions of the cross,
text:
Of those that stand alone,
Still fascinated to presume
That some are like my own.

title: Poem 1477
text: XXXIV.

title: I have a king who does not speak;
text:
So, wondering, thro' the hours meek
I trudge the day away,–
Half glad when it is night and sleep,
If, haply, thro' a dream to peep
In parlors shut by day.

title: And if I do, when morning comes,
text:
It is as if a hundred drums
Did round my pillow roll,
And shouts fill all my childish sky,
And bells keep saying 'victory'
From steeples in my soul!

title: And if I don't, the little Bird
text:
Within the Orchard is not heard,
And I omit to pray,
'Father, thy will be done' to–day,
For my will goes the other way,
And it were perjury!

title: Poem 1481
text: XXXV.

title: Poem 1482
text: DISENCHANTMENT.

title: It dropped so low in my regard
Yet blamed the fate that fractured, less
text:
Than I reviled myself
For entertaining plated wares
Upon my silver shelf.

title: Poem 1485
text: XXXVI.

title: Poem 1486
text: LOST FAITH.

title: To lose one's faith surpasses
text:
The loss of an estate,
Because estates can be
Replenished, – faith cannot.

title: Inherited with life,
Poem 1489
text: XXXVII.

title: Poem 1490
text: LOST JOY.

title: I had a daily bliss
text:
I half indifferent viewed,
Till sudden I perceived it stir, –
It grew as I pursued,

title: Till when, around a crag,
text:
It wasted from my sight,
Enlarged beyond my utmost scope,
I learned its sweetness right.

title: Poem 1493
text: XXXVIII.

title: I worked for chaff, and earning wheat
text:
Was haughty and betrayed.
What right had fields to arbitrate
In matters ratified?

title: I tasted wheat, – and hated chaff,
Poem 1496
text: XXXIX.

title: Life, and Death, and Giants
text:
Such as these, are still.
Minor apparatus, hopper of the mill,
Beetle at the candle,
Or a fife's small fame,
Maintain by accident
That they proclaim.

title: Poem 1498
text: XL.

title: Poem 1499
text: ALPINE GLOW.

title: Our lives are Swiss, –
text:
So still, so cool,
Till, some odd afternoon,
The Alps neglect their curtains,
And we look farther on.

title: Italy stands the other side,
text:
While, like a guard between,
The solemn Alps,
The siren Alps,
Forever intervene!

title: Poem 1502
text: XLI.

title: Poem 1503
text: REMEMBRANCE.

title: Remembrance has a rear and front, –
Besides, the deepest cellar
Poem 1506
text: XLII.

title: To hang our head ostensibly,
text:
And subsequent to find
That such was not the posture
Of our immortal mind,

title: Affords the sly presumption
text:
That, in so dense a fuzz,
You, too, take cobweb attitudes
Upon a plane of gauze!

title: Poem 1509
text: XLIII.

title: Poem 1510
text: THE BRAIN.

title: The brain is wider than the sky,
text:
For, put them side by side,
The one the other will include
With ease, and you beside.

title: The brain is deeper than the sea,
text:
For, hold them, blue to blue,
The one the other will absorb,
As sponges, buckets do.

title: The brain is just the weight of God,
text:
For, lift them, pound for pound,
And they will differ, if they do,
As syllable from sound.

title: Poem 1514
text: XLIV.

title: The bone that has no marrow;
text:
What ultimate for that?
It is not fit for table,
For beggar, or for cat.

title: A bone has obligations,
But how shall finished creatures
text:
A function fresh obtain? –
Old Nicodemus' phantom
Confronting us again!

title: Poem 1518
text: XLV.

title: Poem 1519
text: THE PAST.

title: The past is such a curious creature,
text:
To look her in the face
A transport may reward us,
Or a disgrace.

title: Unarmed if any meet her,
text:
I charge him, fly!
Her rusty ammunition
Might yet reply!

title: Poem 1522
text: XLVI.

title: To help our bleaker parts
text:
Salubrious hours are given,
Which if they do not fit for earth
Drill silently for heaven.

title: Poem 1524
text: XLVII.

title: What soft, cherubic creatures
text:
These gentlewomen are!
One would as soon assault a plush
Or violate a star.

title: Such dimity convictions,
text:
A horror so refined
Of freckled human nature,
Of Deity ashamed, –

title: It's such a common glory,
text:
A fisherman's degree!
Redemption, brittle lady,
Be so, ashamed of thee.

title: Poem 1528
text: XLVIII.

title: Poem 1529
text: DESIRE.

title: Who never wanted, – maddest joy
text:
Remains to him unknown:
The banquet of abstemiousness
Surpasses that of wine.

title: Within its hope, though yet ungrasped
text:
Desire's perfect goal,
No nearer, lest reality
Should disenthrall thy soul.

title: Poem 1532
text: XLIX.

title: Poem 1533
text: PHILOSOPHY.

title: It might be easier
text:
To fail with land in sight,
Than gain my blue peninsula
To perish of delight.

title: Poem 1535
text: L.

title: Poem 1536
text: POWER.

title: You cannot put a fire out;
text:
A thing that can ignite
Can go, itself, without a fan
Upon the slowest night.

title: You cannot fold a flood
text:
And put it in a drawer, –
Because the winds would find it out,
And tell your cedar floor.

title: Poem 1539
text: LI.

title: A modest lot, a fame petite,
text:
A brief campaign of sting and sweet
Is plenty! Is enough!
A sailor's business is the shore,
A soldier's – balls. Who asketh more
Must seek the neighboring life!

title: Poem 1541
text: LII.

title: Is bliss, then, such abyss
text:
I must not put my foot amiss
For fear I spoil my shoe?

title: I'd rather suit my foot
text:
Than save my boot,
For yet to buy another pair
Is possible
At any fair.

title: But bliss is sold just once;
text:
The patent lost
None buy it any more.

title: Poem 1545
text: LIII.

title: Poem 1546
text: EXPERIENCE.

title: I stepped from plank to plank
I knew not but the next
text:
Would be my final inch, –
This gave me that precarious gait
Some call experience.

title: Poem 1549
text: LIV.

title: Poem 1550
text: THANKSGIVING DAY.

title: One day is there of the series
text:
Termed Thanksgiving day,
Celebrated part at table,
Part in memory.

title: Neither patriarch nor pussy,
Had there been no sharp subtraction
text:
From the early sum,
Not an acre or a caption
Where was once a room,

title: Not a mention, whose small pebble
text:
Wrinkled any bay, –
Unto such, were such assembly,
'T were Thanksgiving day.

title: Poem 1555
text: LV.

title: Poem 1556
text: CHILDISH GRIEFS.

title: Softened by Time's consummate plush,
text:
How sleek the woe appears
That threatened childhood's citadel
And undermined the years!

title: Bisected now by bleaker griefs,
text:
We envy the despair
That devastated childhood's realm,
So easy to repair.

title: Poem 1559
text: II. LOVE.

title: Poem 1560
text: I.

title: Poem 1561
text: CONSECRATION.

title: Proud of my broken heart since thou didst break it,
text:
Proud of the pain I did not feel till thee,
Proud of my night since thou with moons dost slake it,
Not to partake thy passion, my humility.

title: Poem 1563
text: II.

title: Poem 1564
text: LOVE'S HUMILITY.

title: My worthiness is all my doubt,
Lest I should insufficient prove
text:
For his beloved need,
The chiefest apprehension
Within my loving creed.

title: So I, the undivine abode
text:
Of his elect content,
Conform my soul as 't were a church
Unto her sacrament.

title: Poem 1568
text: III.

title: Poem 1569
text: LOVE.

title: Love is anterior to life,
text:
Posterior to death,
Initial of creation, and
The exponent of breath.

title: Poem 1571
text: IV.

title: Poem 1572
text: SATISFIED.

title: One blessing had I, than the rest
text:
So larger to my eyes
That I stopped gauging, satisfied,
For this enchanted size.

title: It was the limit of my dream,
text:
The focus of my prayer, –
A perfect, paralyzing bliss
Contented as despair.

title: I knew no more of want or cold,
text:
Phantasms both become,
For this new value in the soul,
Supremest earthly sum.

title: The heaven below the heaven above
Why joys so scantily disburse,
text:
Why Paradise defer,
Why floods are served to us in bowls, –
I speculate no more.

title: Poem 1578
text: V.

title: Poem 1579
text: WITH A FLOWER.

title: When roses cease to bloom, dear,
text:
And violets are done,
When bumble–bees in solemn flight
Have passed beyond the sun,

title: The hand that paused to gather
text:
Upon this summer's day
Will idle lie, in Auburn, –
Then take my flower, pray!

title: Poem 1582
text: VI.

title: Poem 1583
text: SONG.

title: Summer for thee grant I may be
text:
When summer days are flown!
Thy music still when whippoorwill
And oriole are done!

title: For thee to bloom, I'll skip the tomb
text:
And sow my blossoms o'er!
Pray gather me, Anemone,
Thy flower forevermore!

title: Poem 1586
text: VII.

title: Poem 1587
text: LOYALTY.

title: Split the lark and you'll find the music,
text:
Bulb after bulb, in silver rolled,
Scantily dealt to the summer morning,
Saved for your ear when lutes be old.

title: Loose the flood, you shall find it patent,
Poem 1590
text: VIII.

title: To lose thee, sweeter than to gain
text:
All other hearts I knew.
'T is true the drought is destitute,
But then I had the dew!

title: The Caspian has its realms of sand,
Poem 1593
text: IX.

title: Poor little heart!
text:
Did they forget thee?
Then dinna care! Then dinna care!

title: Proud little heart!
text:
Did they forsake thee?
Be debonair! Be debonair!

title: Frail little heart!
text:
I would not break thee:
Could'st credit me? Could'st credit me?

title: Gay little heart!
text:
Like morning glory
Thou'll wilted be; thou'll wilted be!

title: Poem 1598
text: X.

title: Poem 1599
text: FORGOTTEN.

title: There is a word
text:
Which bears a sword
Can pierce an armed man.
It hurls its barbed syllables,–
At once is mute again.
But where it fell
The saved will tell
On patriotic day,
Some epauletted brother
Gave his breath away.

title: Wherever runs the breathless sun,
text:
Wherever roams the day,
There is its noiseless onset,
There is its victory!

title: Behold the keenest marksman!
text:
The most accomplished shot!
Time's sublimest target
Is a soul 'forgot'!

title: Poem 1603
text: XI.

title: I've got an arrow here;
text:
Loving the hand that sent it,
I the dart revere.

title: Fell, they will say, in 'skirmish'!
text:
Vanquished, my soul will know,
By but a simple arrow
Sped by an archer's bow.

title: Poem 1606
text: XII.

title: Poem 1607
text: THE MASTER.

title: He fumbles at your spirit
Prepares your brittle substance
text:
For the ethereal blow,
By fainter hammers, further heard,
Then nearer, then so slow

title: Your breath has time to straighten,
text:
Your brain to bubble cool, –
Deals one imperial thunderbolt
That scalps your naked soul.

title: Poem 1611
text: XIII.

title: Heart, we will forget him!
text:
You and I, to–night!
You may forget the warmth he gave,
I will forget the light.

title: When you have done, pray tell me,
Poem 1614
text: XIV.

title: Father, I bring thee not myself, –
The heart I cherished in my own
text:
Till mine too heavy grew,
Yet strangest, heavier since it went,
Is it too large for you?

title: Poem 1617
text: XV.

title: We outgrow love like other things
text:
And put it in the drawer,
Till it an antique fashion shows
Like costumes grandsires wore.

title: Poem 1619
text: XVI.

title: Not with a club the heart is broken,
To lash the magic creature
text:
Till it fell,
Yet that whip's name too noble
Then to tell.

title: Magnanimous of bird
text:
By boy descried,
To sing unto the stone
Of which it died.

title: Poem 1623
text: XVII.

title: Poem 1624
text: WHO?

title: My friend must be a bird,
text:
Because it flies!
Mortal my friend must be,
Because it dies!
Barbs has it, like a bee.
Ah, curious friend,
Thou puzzlest me!

title: Poem 1626
text: XVIII.

title: He touched me, so I live to know
text:
That such a day, permitted so,
I groped upon his breast.
It was a boundless place to me,
And silenced, as the awful sea
Puts minor streams to rest.

title: And now, I'm different from before,
text:
As if I breathed superior air,
Or brushed a royal gown;
My feet, too, that had wandered so,
My gypsy face transfigured now
To tenderer renown.

title: Poem 1629
text: XIX.

title: Poem 1630
text: DREAMS.

title: Let me not mar that perfect dream
text:
By an auroral stain,
But so adjust my daily night
That it will come again.

title: Poem 1632
text: XX.

title: Poem 1633
text: NUMEN LUMEN.

title: I live with him, I see his face;
The only one forestalling mine,
text:
And that by right that he
Presents a claim invisible,
No wedlock granted me.

title: I live with him, I hear his voice,
text:
I stand alive to–day
To witness to the certainty
Of immortality

title: Taught me by Time, – the lower way,
text:
Conviction every day, –
That life like this is endless,
Be judgment what it may.

title: Poem 1638
text: XXI.

title: Poem 1639
text: LONGING.

title: I envy seas whereon he rides,
text:
I envy spokes of wheels
Of chariots that him convey,
I envy speechless hills

title: That gaze upon his journey;
text:
How easy all can see
What is forbidden utterly
As heaven, unto me!

title: I envy nests of sparrows
text:
That dot his distant eaves,
The wealthy fly upon his pane,
The happy, happy leaves

title: That just abroad his window
text:
Have summer's leave to be,
The earrings of Pizarro
Could not obtain for me.

title: I envy light that wakes him,
text:
And bells that boldly ring
To tell him it is noon abroad, –
Myself his noon could bring,

title: Yet interdict my blossom
text:
And abrogate my bee,
Lest noon in everlasting night
Drop Gabriel and me.

title: Poem 1646
text: XXII.

title: Poem 1647
text: WEDDED.

title: A solemn thing it was, I said,
text:
A woman white to be,
And wear, if God should count me fit,
Her hallowed mystery.

title: A timid thing to drop a life
text:
Into the purple well,
Too plummetless that it come back
Eternity until.

title: Poem 1650
text: III. NATURE.

title: Poem 1651
text: I.

title: Poem 1652
text: NATURE'S CHANGES.

title: The springtime's pallid landscape
text:
Will glow like bright bouquet,
Though drifted deep in parian
The village lies to–day.

title: The lilacs, bending many a year,
The rose will redden in the bog,
text:
The aster on the hill
Her everlasting fashion set,
And covenant gentians frill,

title: Till summer folds her miracle
text:
As women do their gown,
Or priests adjust the symbols
When sacrament is done.

title: Poem 1657
text: II.

title: Poem 1658
text: THE TULIP.

title: She slept beneath a tree
text:
Remembered but by me.
I touched her cradle mute;
She recognized the foot,
Put on her carmine suit, –
And see!

title: Poem 1660
text: III.

title: A light exists in spring
text:
Not present on the year
At any other period.
When March is scarcely here

title: A color stands abroad
text:
On solitary hills
That science cannot overtake,
But human nature feels.

title: It waits upon the lawn;
Then, as horizons step,
text:
Or noons report away,
Without the formula of sound,
It passes, and we stay:

title: A quality of loss
text:
Affecting our content,
As trade had suddenly encroached
Upon a sacrament.

title: Poem 1666
text: IV.

title: Poem 1667
text: THE WAKING YEAR.

title: A lady red upon the hill
The tidy breezes with their brooms
text:
Sweep vale, and hill, and tree!
Prithee, my pretty housewives!
Who may expected be?

title: The neighbors do not yet suspect!
text:
The woods exchange a smile –
Orchard, and buttercup, and bird –
In such a little while!

title: And yet how still the landscape stands,
text:
How nonchalant the wood,
As if the resurrection
Were nothing very odd!

title: Poem 1672
text: V.

title: Poem 1673
text: TO MARCH.

title: Dear March, come in!
text:
How glad I am!
I looked for you before.
Put down your hat –
You must have walked –
How out of breath you are!
Dear March, how are you?
And the rest?
Did you leave Nature well?
Oh, March, come right upstairs with me,
I have so much to tell!

title: I got your letter, and the birds';
text:
The maples never knew
That you were coming, – I declare,
How red their faces grew!
But, March, forgive me –
And all those hills
You left for me to hue;
There was no purple suitable,
You took it all with you.

title: Who knocks? That April!
text:
Lock the door!
I will not be pursued!
He stayed away a year, to call
When I am occupied.
But trifles look so trivial
As soon as you have come,
That blame is just as dear as praise
And praise as mere as blame.

title: Poem 1677
text: VI.

title: Poem 1678
text: MARCH.

title: We like March, his shoes are purple,
text:
He is new and high;
Makes he mud for dog and peddler,
Makes he forest dry;
Knows the adder's tongue his coming,
And begets her spot.
Stands the sun so close and mighty
That our minds are hot.
News is he of all the others;
Bold it were to die
With the blue–birds buccaneering
On his British sky.

title: Poem 1680
text: VII.

title: Poem 1681
text: DAWN.

title: Not knowing when the dawn will come
Poem 1683
text: VIII.

title: A murmur in the trees to note,
A long, long yellow on the lawn,
A hurrying home of little men
text:
To houses unperceived, –
All this, and more, if I should tell,
Would never be believed.

title: Of robins in the trundle bed
text:
How many I espy
Whose nightgowns could not hide the wings,
Although I heard them try!

title: But then I promised ne'er to tell;
text:
How could I break my word?
So go your way and I'll go mine, –
No fear you'll miss the road.

title: Poem 1689
text: IX.

title: Morning is the place for dew,
text:
Corn is made at noon,
After dinner light for flowers,
Dukes for setting sun!

title: Poem 1691
text: X.

title: To my quick ear the leaves conferred;
In cave if I presumed to hide,
Poem 1694
text: XI.

title: Poem 1695
text: A ROSE.

title: A sepal, petal, and a thorn
text:
Upon a common summer's morn,
A flash of dew, a bee or two,
A breeze
A caper in the trees, –
And I'm a rose!

title: Poem 1697
text: XII.

title: High from the earth I heard a bird;
text:
He trod upon the trees
As he esteemed them trifles,
And then he spied a breeze,
And situated softly
Upon a pile of wind
Which in a perturbation
Nature had left behind.
A joyous–going fellow
I gathered from his talk,
Which both of benediction
And badinage partook,
Without apparent burden,
I learned, in leafy wood
He was the faithful father
Of a dependent brood;
And this untoward transport
His remedy for care, –
A contrast to our respites.
How different we are!

title: Poem 1699
text: XIII.

title: Poem 1700
text: COBWEBS.

title: The spider as an artist
text:
Has never been employed
Though his surpassing merit
Is freely certified

title: By every broom and Bridget
text:
Throughout a Christian land.
Neglected son of genius,
I take thee by the hand.

title: Poem 1703
text: XIV.

title: Poem 1704
text: A WELL.

title: What mystery pervades a well!
text:
The water lives so far,
Like neighbor from another world
Residing in a jar.

title: The grass does not appear afraid;
text:
I often wonder he
Can stand so close and look so bold
At what is dread to me.

title: Related somehow they may be, –
text:
The sedge stands next the sea,
Where he is floorless, yet of fear
No evidence gives he.

title: But nature is a stranger yet;
text:
The ones that cite her most
Have never passed her haunted house,
Nor simplified her ghost.

title: To pity those that know her not
text:
Is helped by the regret
That those who know her, know her less
The nearer her they get.

title: Poem 1710
text: XV.

title: To make a prairie it takes a clover and one bee, –
text:
One clover, and a bee,
And revery.
The revery alone will do
If bees are few.

title: Poem 1712
text: XVI.

title: Poem 1713
text: THE WIND.

title: It's like the light, –
text:
A fashionless delight
It's like the bee, –
A dateless melody.

title: It's like the woods,
text:
Private like breeze,
Phraseless, yet it stirs
The proudest trees.

title: It's like the morning, –
text:
Best when it's done, –
The everlasting clocks
Chime noon.

title: Poem 1717
text: XVII.

title: A dew sufficed itself
text:
And satisfied a leaf,
And felt, 'how vast a destiny!
How trivial is life!'

title: The sun went out to work,
text:
The day went out to play,
But not again that dew was seen
By physiognomy.

title: Whether by day abducted,
text:
Or emptied by the sun
Into the sea, in passing,
Eternally unknown.

title: Poem 1721
text: XVIII.

title: Poem 1722
text: THE WOODPECKER.

title: His bill an auger is,
text:
His head, a cap and frill.
He laboreth at every tree, –
A worm his utmost goal.

title: Poem 1724
text: XIX.

title: Poem 1725
text: A SNAKE.

title: Sweet is the swamp with its secrets,
text:
Until we meet a snake;
'T is then we sigh for houses,
And our departure take
At that enthralling gallop
That only childhood knows.
A snake is summer's treason,
And guile is where it goes.

title: Poem 1727
text: XX.

title: Could I but ride indefinite,
text:
As doth the meadow–bee,
And visit only where I liked,
And no man visit me,

title: And flirt all day with buttercups,
text:
And marry whom I may,
And dwell a little everywhere,
Or better, run away

title: With no police to follow,
text:
Or chase me if I do,
Till I should jump peninsulas
To get away from you, –

title: I said, but just to be a bee
text:
Upon a raft of air,
And row in nowhere all day long,
And anchor off the bar,–
What liberty! So captives deem
Who tight in dungeons are.

title: Poem 1732
text: XXI.

title: Poem 1733
text: THE MOON.

title: The moon was but a chin of gold
text:
A night or two ago,
And now she turns her perfect face
Upon the world below.

title: Her forehead is of amplest blond;
Her lips of amber never part;
text:
But what must be the smile
Upon her friend she could bestow
Were such her silver will!

title: And what a privilege to be
text:
But the remotest star!
For certainly her way might pass
Beside your twinkling door.

title: Her bonnet is the firmament,
text:
The universe her shoe,
The stars the trinkets at her belt,
Her dimities of blue.

title: Poem 1739
text: XXII.

title: Poem 1740
text: THE BAT.

title: The bat is dun with wrinkled wings
text:
Like fallow article,
And not a song pervades his lips,
Or none perceptible.

title: His small umbrella, quaintly halved,
text:
Describing in the air
An arc alike inscrutable, –
Elate philosopher!

title: Deputed from what firmament
text:
Of what astute abode,
Empowered with what malevolence
Auspiciously withheld.

title: To his adroit Creator
Poem 1745
text: XXIII.

title: Poem 1746
text: THE BALLOON.

title: You've seen balloons set, haven't you?
text:
So stately they ascend
It is as swans discarded you
For duties diamond.

title: Their liquid feet go softly out
Their ribbons just beyond the eye,
The gilded creature strains and spins,
text:
Trips frantic in a tree,
Tears open her imperial veins
And tumbles in the sea.

title: The crowd retire with an oath
text:
The dust in streets goes down,
And clerks in counting–rooms observe,
''T was only a balloon.'

title: Poem 1752
text: XXIV.

title: Poem 1753
text: EVENING.

title: The cricket sang,
text:
And set the sun,
And workmen finished, one by one,
Their seam the day upon.

title: The low grass loaded with the dew,
text:
The twilight stood as strangers do
With hat in hand, polite and new,
To stay as if, or go.

title: A vastness, as a neighbor, came, –
text:
A wisdom without face or name,
A peace, as hemispheres at home, –
And so the night became.

title: Poem 1757
text: XXV.

title: Poem 1758
text: COCOON.

title: Drab habitation of whom?
text:
Tabernacle or tomb,
Or dome of worm,
Or porch of gnome,
Or some elf's catacomb?

title: Poem 1760
text: XXVI.

title: Poem 1761
text: SUNSET.

title: A sloop of amber slips away
text:
Upon an ether sea,
And wrecks in peace a purple tar,
The son of ecstasy.

title: Poem 1763
text: XXVII.

title: Poem 1764
text: AURORA.

title: Of bronze and blaze
text:
The north, to–night!
So adequate its forms,
So preconcerted with itself,
So distant to alarms, –
An unconcern so sovereign
To universe, or me,
It paints my simple spirit
With tints of majesty,
Till I take vaster attitudes,
And strut upon my stem,
Disdaining men and oxygen,
For arrogance of them.

title: My splendors are menagerie;
text:
But their competeless show
Will entertain the centuries
When I am, long ago,
An island in dishonored grass,
Whom none but daisies know.

title: Poem 1767
text: XXVIII.

title: Poem 1768
text: THE COMING OF NIGHT.

title: How the old mountains drip with sunset,
text:
And the brake of dun!
How the hemlocks are tipped in tinsel
By the wizard sun!

title: How the old steeples hand the scarlet,
text:
Till the ball is full, –
Have I the lip of the flamingo
That I dare to tell?

title: Then, how the fire ebbs like billows,
text:
Touching all the grass
With a departing, sapphire feature,
As if a duchess pass!

title: How a small dusk crawls on the village
Now it is night in nest and kennel,
text:
And where was the wood,
Just a dome of abyss is nodding
Into solitude! –

title: These are the visions baffled Guido;
Poem 1775
text: XXIX.

title: Poem 1776
text: AFTERMATH.

title: The murmuring of bees has ceased;
text:
But murmuring of some
Posterior, prophetic,
Has simultaneous come, –

title: The lower metres of the year,
text:
When nature's laugh is done, –
The Revelations of the book
Whose Genesis is June.

title: Poem 1779
text: IV. TIME AND ETERNITY.

title: Poem 1780
text: I.

title: This world is not conclusion;
text:
A sequel stands beyond,
Invisible, as music,
But positive, as sound.
It beckons and it baffles;
Philosophies don't know,
And through a riddle, at the last,
Sagacity must go.
To guess it puzzles scholars;
To gain it, men have shown
Contempt of generations,
And crucifixion known.

title: Poem 1782
text: II.

title: We learn in the retreating
text:
How vast an one
Was recently among us.
A perished sun

title: Endears in the departure
text:
How doubly more
Than all the golden presence
It was before!

title: Poem 1785
text: III.

title: They say that 'time assuages,' –
